<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Foundational Data Structures - Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1. Foundational Data Structures

A **Foundational Data Structure** is a systematic way of organizing, storing, and managing data in main memory (RAM) so that it can be accessed and modified efficiently by algorithms. In KTU's *Advanced Data Structures* framework, foundational structures form the **building blocks** upon which complex structures (like B-Trees, Red-Black Trees, Fibonacci Heaps) are layered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** A student must demonstrate mastery of arrays, linked lists, pointer-object emulation, tree representations, and hashing (including **Cuckoo Hashing**) before progressing to advanced topics. These form the **base layer** of the course.

---

## 2. Overview of Arrays

### Formal Definition
An **Array** is a contiguous block of memory locations that store homogeneous elements (same data type) and supports **constant-time random access** via index arithmetic.

$$ \text{Address of } A[i] = \text{Base Address} + i \times \text{sizeof(element)} $$

### Intuitive Analogy
> Imagine a **movie theatre row of seats numbered 1 to 20**. If you know the seat number, you can walk straight to it in one step — that is random access. But inserting a new person in the middle requires everyone to shift — that is the cost of insertion.

### Key Properties
- **Memory Layout:** *Contiguous*
- **Access Time:** $O(1)$
- **Search Time (unsorted):** $O(n)$
- **Insertion/Deletion (worst case):** $O(n)$
- **Cache Friendliness:** Excellent (spatial locality)

---

## 3. Overview of Linked Lists

### Formal Definition
A **Linked List** is a linear data structure where elements (**nodes**) are stored in *arbitrary memory locations* and connected using **pointers**. Each node contains the data and a pointer (or pointers) to the next/previous node.

### Intuitive Analogy
> Think of a **treasure hunt** where each clue tells you the location of the next clue. To find clue $k$, you must walk through clues $1, 2, \ldots, k-1$ first. Insertion is easy — just change two pointers — but finding the $k$-th element requires a sequential walk.

### Variants
| Variant | Pointers per Node | Traversal |
|---|---|---|
| Singly Linked | `next` | Forward only |
| Doubly Linked | `next`, `prev` | Forward + Backward |
| Circular | `next` (last → first) | Cyclic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Static vs Dynamic:** Arrays are *static-friendly* (size known at compile time), while linked lists are *dynamic-friendly* (grow/shrink at runtime). The trade-off is access-speed vs flexibility.

---

## 4. Implementation of Pointers and Objects

In languages lacking true pointer support (or when working at a low level), we can **simulate pointers using arrays**. Each object is stored in an array, and a "pointer" is simply an **index (integer)** into that array.

### The Multi-Array Technique

$$ \text{Object } i \;\; \equiv \;\; \text{Three parallel arrays: } Key[i], \; Next[i], \; Prev[i] $$

A "pointer to object $i$" is just the integer $i$ itself.

### Intuitive Analogy
> Imagine a **rolodex** where each contact card has an ID number. Instead of physically handing someone the card, you just say "look up card #42" — that number **IS** the pointer.

### Advantages of Pointer Emulation
- **Garbage Collection** can be done explicitly (sweep phase frees index)
- Faster allocation/deallocation (no system calls)
- Compact memory layout (better cache usage)
- Useful in **kernel programming** and **embedded systems**

---

## 5. Representing Rooted Trees

A **Rooted Tree** is a hierarchical, acyclic structure with a distinguished node (the *root*) from which all other nodes descend.

### Common Representation Schemes

#### (a) Left-Child / Right-Sibling (LCRS) Representation
The most space-efficient *general* tree representation using only **two pointers per node**:
- `left-child` → first child
- `right-sibling` → next sibling

#### (b) Binary Tree Representation
Direct representation where each node has `left` and `right` pointers. A general tree with $n$ nodes uses $n$ nodes, each with two pointers — but many are `NIL`.

#### (c) Parent Pointer Array
A single array $P[1 \dots n]$ where $P[i]$ stores the index of the parent of node $i$. The root has $P[\text{root}] = 0$ (or `NIL`).

### Space Complexity
For a tree with $n$ nodes:
$$ \text{LCRS Memory} = O(n) \text{ (just 2 pointers per node)} $$
$$ \text{Binary representation} = O(n) \text{ (also 2 pointers, but wastes NIL)} $$
$$ \text{Parent pointer array} = O(n) \text{ (single integer per node)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The Left-Child / Right-Sibling representation is the *canonical* way to convert a general tree into a binary tree. This is a frequently asked 7-mark question.

---

## 6. Hashing - Hash Tables

### Formal Definition
A **Hash Table** is a data structure that maps keys to array indices using a **hash function**, achieving average-case $O(1)$ insert, delete, and search — making it one of the most practically important structures in computer science.

### Intuitive Analogy
> Think of a **library** where books are placed on shelves based on the **last digit of their accession number**. You don't search all shelves — you compute the shelf number and walk straight to it. That's hashing.

### Components of a Hashing System
$$ \text{Hash System} = \underbrace{h(k)}_{\text{Hash Function}} \;\; + \;\; \underbrace{T[0 \dots m-1]}_{\text{Hash Table}} \;\; + \;\; \underbrace{\text{Collision Strategy}}_{\text{Chaining / Open Addressing}} $$

### Direct-Address Tables (Conceptual Parent)
For a universe $U = \{0, 1, \dots, u-1\}$ and an actual key set $K$, a direct-address table is an array $T[0 \dots u-1]$ where each slot either holds a pointer to an element or `NIL`. Drawback: $O(\vert U \vert)$ space, often impractical.

---

## 7. Hash Functions

A **Hash Function** $h: U \rightarrow \{0, 1, \dots, m-1\}$ maps keys to table slots. A *good* hash function should distribute keys **uniformly** across all $m$ slots.

### Desirable Properties of a Good Hash Function (KTU Board Examiner's Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Deterministic** — Same key $\Rightarrow$ same slot every time
> 2. **Uniform Distribution** — Every slot equally likely
> 3. **Fast to Compute** — $O(1)$ evaluation
> 4. **Minimize Collisions** — Distinct keys should map to distinct slots where possible
> 5. **Avalanche Effect** — Tiny input change $\Rightarrow$ large output change

### Three Classic Hash Functions

#### Division Method
$$ h(k) = k \bmod m $$
Choose $m$ = **prime number, not too close to a power of 2**.

#### Multiplication Method
$$ h(k) = \left\lfloor m \cdot \big( kA \bmod 1 \big) \right\rfloor $$
Constant $A \in (0,1)$; Knuth suggests $A \approx (\sqrt{5}-1)/2 \approx 0.6180339887$ (golden ratio).

#### Universal Hashing
$$ h_{a,b}(k) = \big( (a \cdot k + b) \bmod p \big) \bmod m $$
with prime $p > \vert U \vert$, $a \in \{1,\dots,p-1\}$, $b \in \{0,\dots,p-1\}$.

---

## 8. Cuckoo Hashing

### Formal Definition
**Cuckoo Hashing** is a collision-resolution scheme using **two hash functions** $h_1$ and $h_2$ and **two tables** $T_1, T_2$. Every key resides in **exactly one** of two possible locations: $T_1[h_1(k)]$ or $T_2[h_2(k)]$. On insertion, if the target slot is occupied, the existing key is **kicked out (evicted)** and re-inserted into its alternate location — recursively.

### Intuitive Analogy
> Imagine two pigeonholes for every item. If both are full, evict the current occupant and place it in **its other** pigeonhole. This **bouncing** (like a cuckoo bird pushing eggs out of nests) continues until every item finds a home — or a cycle is detected.

### Key Properties (for the KTU Board)
> [!NOTE]
> - **Lookup Time:** $O(1)$ **worst case** (only 2 probes!)
> - **Insertion Time:** $O(1)$ **expected**, but may loop
> - **Space:** $O(n)$ for two tables of size $\geq 2n$
> - **Cycle Detection:** If insertion loops, the table must be rebuilt (rehash) with new hash functions

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 1. Arrays - Theoretical Deep Dive

### Memory Address Computation
For an array $A$ declared in C as `int A[n]`, with base address $B$:
$$ \text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + i \cdot w $$
where $w$ is the **word size** in bytes (here $w = 4$ for `int` on a 32-bit system).

### Row-Major vs Column-Major (Multidimensional)
For a 2-D array $A[m][n]$ in **row-major** form:
$$ \text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (i \cdot n + j) \cdot w $$
For **column-major** form (used in FORTRAN):
$$ \text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (j \cdot m + i) \cdot w $$

### Strengths & Weaknesses
- ✅ **Strengths:** $O(1)$ random access; cache-friendly; simple implementation
- ❌ **Weaknesses:** Fixed size at allocation; $O(n)$ worst-case insertion; wasted memory if underutilized

---

## 2. Linked Lists - Theoretical Deep Dive

### Operations and Complexities
| Operation | Singly LL | Doubly LL |
|---|---|---|
| Insert at head | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insert at tail | $O(n)$ or $O(1)$ with tail ptr | $O(1)$ with tail ptr |
| Delete head | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Delete given node | $O(n)$ (need prev) | $O(1)$ |
| Search | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |

### Why "Doubly" Costs More
A doubly-linked node stores **2 pointers + data**, increasing the memory per node from $O(1)$ overhead to roughly $2\times$ the pointer overhead. However, it enables **bidirectional traversal** and **$O(1)$ deletion** given a node reference.

---

## 3. Implementation of Pointers and Objects - Multi-Array Scheme

### The Three-Parallel-Arrays Trick
For representing a doubly-linked list with $n$ objects in arrays:

$$ Key[1 \dots n], \quad Next[1 \dots n], \quad Prev[1 \dots n] $$

A "pointer to object $x$" is the integer index $x$ itself.

### Allocating a New Object
Maintain a free list:
$$ \text{free} \rightarrow \text{first available index} $$

$$ x = \text{free}; \quad \text{free} = Next[\text{free}] $$

### Garbage Collection (Mark-and-Sweep in Emulated Form)
The **Mark phase** traverses the reachable object graph and sets a `mark[i]` flag. The **Sweep phase** walks the entire array, and for every $i$ where `mark[i] == false`, links `Next[i] = free; free = i`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This is the exact mechanism used by the **Mark-Sweep GC of Java Virtual Machines** (early HotSpot, JVM with `UseSerialGC`). Many language runtimes (Lisp, early Python) historically used this representation.

---

## 4. Representing Rooted Trees - Theoretical Deep Dive

### Comparison of Representations

| Representation | Memory per Node | Compute Parent | Compute Children |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Left-Child / Right-Sibling** | 2 pointers | $O(\text{depth})$ | $O(\text{degree})$ |
| **Binary Tree (direct)** | 2 pointers | $O(\text{depth})$ | $O(\text{degree})$ |
| **Parent Pointer Array** | 1 integer | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ scan |
| **Adjacency Matrix** | $n^2$ bits | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ scan |

### LCRS Pseudostructure
```text
struct LCRSTreeNode {
    T key;
    int left_child;    // pointer/index to first child
    int right_sibling; // pointer/index to next sibling
};
```

### Why LCRS is Preferred
- Uses exactly **2 pointers per node** (same as a binary tree).
- Any **general tree** can be uniquely mapped to a **binary tree** — enabling the use of binary tree algorithms on general trees.

---

## 5. Hash Tables - Theoretical Deep Dive

### Load Factor (Critical KTU Concept)
$$ \alpha = \frac{n}{m} $$
where $n$ = number of stored keys, $m$ = number of slots. $\alpha$ controls the **trade-off between space and time**.

### Average-Case Costs
| Collision Strategy | Successful Search | Unsuccessful Search |
|---|---|---|
| Chaining | $1 + \alpha/2$ | $1 + \alpha$ |
| Linear Probing | $\frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{1-\alpha}\right)$ | $\frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{(1-\alpha)^2}\right)$ |
| Quadratic Probing | $-\frac{1}{\alpha}\ln(1-\alpha)$ | $\frac{1}{1-\alpha}$ |
| Double Hashing | $-\frac{1}{\alpha}\ln(1-\alpha)$ | $\frac{1}{1-\alpha}$ |

### Resolving Collisions
1. **Chaining** — Each slot holds a linked list (or BST).
2. **Open Addressing** — Probe sequence: $h(k,0), h(k,1), \dots$
   - Linear: $h(k,i) = (h'(k) + i) \bmod m$
   - Quadratic: $h(k,i) = (h'(k) + c_1 i + c_2 i^2) \bmod m$
   - Double Hashing: $h(k,i) = (h_1(k) + i \cdot h_2(k)) \bmod m$

---

## 6. Cuckoo Hashing - Theoretical Deep Dive

### Setup
- Two tables $T_1$ and $T_2$, each of size $m$.
- Two hash functions $h_1: U \to [0, m-1]$ and $h_2: U \to [0, m-1]$.
- **Invariant:** Every key $k$ is either at $T_1[h_1(k)]$ or at $T_2[h_2(k)]$ — **never elsewhere**.

### Lookup
$$ \text{Lookup}(k): \text{check } T_1[h_1(k)] \text{ and } T_2[h_2(k)] \quad \Rightarrow \quad O(1) \text{ worst case} $$

### Insertion Algorithm
1. Loop for `MAX_LOOPS` iterations (commonly `8 \log_2 n`):
2. If $k$ is already in $T_1[h_1(k)]$ or $T_2[h_2(k)]$ — done.
3. Let current table = $T_1$ (alternate $T_1 \leftrightarrow T_2$ on each iteration).
4. If the slot in current table is empty, place $k$ there — done.
5. Otherwise evict the resident key, place $k$, and try to insert the evicted key in its alternate location.

### Cycle Detection & Rehashing
If loops exceed threshold, **rehash** with a new pair $(h_1', h_2')$ and larger tables.

---

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Array address | $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + i \cdot w$ | $w$ = element size in bytes |
| 2 | Row-major 2D | $B + (i \cdot n + j) \cdot w$ | C, C++, Java use this |
| 3 | Column-major 2D | $B + (j \cdot m + i) \cdot w$ | FORTRAN, MATLAB |
| 4 | Load factor | $\alpha = n/m$ | Chaining: $\alpha$ can exceed 1 |
| 5 | Chaining avg search | $O(1 + \alpha)$ | Unsuccessful case |
| 6 | Linear probing failure | $\frac{1}{2}(1 + \frac{1}{(1-\alpha)^2})$ | Diverges as $\alpha \to 1$ |
| 7 | Division method | $h(k) = k \bmod m$ | $m$ prime, $m \not\approx 2^p$ |
| 8 | Multiplication method | $\lfloor m(kA \bmod 1)\rfloor$ | $A \approx 0.618$ (golden) |
| 9 | Universal hashing | $(ak+b)\bmod p \bmod m$ | $p$ prime $> \vert U \vert$ |
| 10 | Cuckoo lookup | $O(1)$ worst-case | **2 probes only** |
| 11 | Cuckoo insert | $O(1)$ expected | $O(\log n)$ worst |
| 12 | Cycle break threshold | $\geq 6 \log_2 n$ (Pagh) | Rehash needed |
| 13 | LCRS space | $2n$ pointers | For $n$ nodes |
| 14 | Parent array space | $n$ integers | Most compact |
| 15 | Multi-array node cost | $3n$ integers | Key + Next + Prev |

---

## Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> - **Hash Tables** power databases (indexed lookups), DNS resolvers, language symbol tables, set/map libraries (C++ `unordered_map`, Python `dict`, Java `HashMap`)
> - **Cuckoo Hashing** is used in **network routers** (Intel DPDK), **memcached**, and **Bloom filter variants** because its $O(1)$ worst-case lookup is critical for real-time packet processing
> - **Linked Lists** are the substrate of OS process/thread schedulers and memory allocators (Linux `malloc` uses intrusive linked lists)
> - **LCRS** underpins Unix filesystem directory trees when stored as binary trees (e.g., early BSD `ufs`)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## Derivation 1: Average Search Cost in Chained Hash Table

We assume **simple uniform hashing**: every key is equally likely to hash into any of the $m$ slots, independently of where other keys hash.

### Setup
Let $n$ keys be stored in a table with $m$ slots. Define the **chain length** at slot $j$ as:
$$ n_j = \vert \{ k : h(k) = j \} \vert $$
$$ \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} n_j = n $$

### Expected Chain Length
By linearity of expectation and uniform hashing:
$$ E[n_j] = \frac{n}{m} = \alpha $$

### Unsuccessful Search Cost
We examine slot $h(k)$ and traverse its entire chain. Cost = chain length:
$$ T_{\text{unsuccessful}} = n_j \quad \Rightarrow \quad E[T_{\text{unsuccessful}}] = E[n_j] = \alpha $$

Including the $O(1)$ hash computation:
$$ E[T_{\text{unsuccessful}}] = 1 + \alpha $$

### Successful Search Cost
A successful search traverses the chain until finding the target. On average, the target is at the **midpoint** of the chain, so:
$$ E[T_{\text{successful}}] = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{2} $$

---

## Derivation 2: Average Probes in Linear Probing

Linear probing uses the probe sequence:
$$ h(k, i) = (h'(k) + i) \bmod m, \quad i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots $$

### Unsuccessful Search
The probability that the **first** probe hits an occupied slot is $\alpha$. Conditional probability for second probe:
$$ P(\text{2nd probed}) = P(\text{1st occupied}) \cdot P(\text{next occupied} \mid \text{1st occupied}) $$

By symmetry (each slot equally likely to be occupied):
$$ P(\text{2nd probed}) = \alpha^2 $$

Generalizing, expected number of probes:
$$ E[T_{\text{unsuccessful}}] = 1 + \alpha + \alpha^2 + \alpha^3 + \cdots = \frac{1}{1-\alpha} $$

### Successful Search
By similar reasoning (and assuming each key equally likely to be the target):
$$ E[T_{\text{successful}}] = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{1-\alpha}\right) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Threshold:** Linear probing degrades sharply as $\alpha \to 1$. Keep $\alpha < 0.7$ to maintain efficiency. KTU examiners often set questions asking what happens at $\alpha = 0.9$.

---

## Derivation 3: Cuckoo Hashing Insertion Analysis

### Setup
Two tables $T_1, T_2$ of size $m$. Two hash functions $h_1, h_2$ chosen **independently at random** from a 2-universal family.

### Invariant After Successful Insertion
For every key $k$ stored:
$$ \big(k \in T_1[h_1(k)]\big) \lor \big(k \in T_2[h_2(k)]\big) $$

### Worst-Case Loop Bound
A single insert performs at most `MAX_LOOP` evictions. If no cycle is encountered within `MAX_LOOP` steps, the insertion succeeds.

The expected number of evictions during a single insertion is:
$$ E[\text{evictions}] = O(1) $$

provided the table's load factor is below a critical threshold:
$$ \alpha_{\text{cuckoo}} < 0.5 \text{ (with 2 hash functions, 2 tables of equal size)} $$

### Successful Insert Probability
For two tables of size $m$ with total load $n$:
$$ P(\text{insertion succeeds in } \leq L \text{ steps}) \geq 1 - O(L \cdot n^3 / m^2) $$

For $m \geq 2n$ (i.e., $\alpha = n/m \leq 0.5$), the failure probability is small.

### Cycle Detection Threshold
Pagh & Rodler (2004) recommend:
$$ \text{MAX\_LOOP} = 6 \log_2 n $$
If exceeded, rehash with new $(h_1', h_2')$ and tables of size $2n$.

---

## Analytical Matrix - Operation Complexity

| Structure | Insert | Delete | Search | Space | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unsorted Array | $O(1)$ at end | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | |
| Sorted Array | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Binary search |
| Singly Linked List | $O(1)$ at head | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | |
| Doubly Linked List | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ given node | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | |
| Hash Chain | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1+\alpha)$ | $O(n+m)$ | |
| Hash Open Addr. | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1/(1-\alpha))$ | $O(m)$ | $\alpha < 0.7$ |
| **Cuckoo Hashing** | $O(1)$ exp. | $O(1)$ worst | $O(1)$ worst | $O(2n)$ | $2$ tables |

---

## Worked Example: Division Method Hashing

Given keys $\{23, 17, 34, 11, 8, 45\}$, table size $m = 7$.

Compute hash for each key using $h(k) = k \bmod 7$:

$$ h(23) = 23 \bmod 7 = 2 $$

$$ h(17) = 17 \bmod 7 = 3 $$

$$ h(34) = 34 \bmod 7 = 6 $$

$$ h(11) = 11 \bmod 7 = 4 $$

$$ h(8) = 8 \bmod 7 = 1 $$

$$ h(45) = 45 \bmod 7 = 3 $$

### Observation: Collision!
Key $17$ and key $45$ both hash to slot $3$. This must be resolved by either chaining (linked list at slot 3) or open addressing (probe next slot).

### Resolved with Linear Probing
- Place $17$ at slot $3$.
- For $45$: slot $3$ occupied, probe slot $4$ — also occupied (by $11$), probe slot $5$ — empty. Place $45$ at slot $5$.

Final table:
| Slot | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Key | — | 8 | 23 | 17 | 11 | 45 | 34 |

---

## Worked Example: Cuckoo Hashing Insertion

Tables $T_1, T_2$ of size $m = 5$. Hash functions:
$$ h_1(k) = k \bmod 5, \quad h_2(k) = \lfloor 5 \cdot (k \cdot 0.6 \bmod 1) \rfloor $$

### Insert $k = 12$
- $h_1(12) = 12 \bmod 5 = 2$. Slot $T_1[2]$ empty. Place at $T_1[2]$. ✓

### Insert $k = 7$
- $h_1(7) = 7 \bmod 5 = 2$. Slot $T_1[2]$ occupied by $12$.
- Evict $12$, place $7$ at $T_1[2]$.
- Re-insert evicted $12$: try $T_2[h_2(12)] = T_2[\lfloor 5 \cdot (7.2 \bmod 1)\rfloor] = T_2[\lfloor 1.0\rfloor] = T_2[1]$.
- $T_2[1]$ empty. Place $12$ at $T_2[1]$. ✓

Final state:
$$ T_1: \;\; [-, -, 7, -, -] $$
$$ T_2: \;\; [-, 12, -, -, -] $$

This shows the *eviction chain* characteristic of cuckoo hashing.

---

## Verification: Load Factor Constraint

For cuckoo hashing with two tables of size $m$ each, total capacity $= 2m$. Safe load factor:
$$ \alpha_{\text{safe}} = \frac{n}{2m} < 0.5 $$

$$ \Rightarrow n < m $$

For 100% reliability, choose $m \geq 2n$. KTU board tip: maintain a rehash trigger at $\alpha \geq 0.4$.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## Diagram 1: Array Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ARR["Array A in Contiguous Memory"]
        A0["A0"]:::arr --- A1["A1"]:::arr --- A2["A2"]:::arr --- A3["A3"]:::arr --- A4["A4"]:::arr
    end
    ADR["Base Address B"] --> A0
    A0 -- "+w" --> A1
    A1 -- "+w" --> A2
    A2 -- "+w" --> A3
    A3 -- "+w" --> A4
    classDef arr fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## Diagram 2: Singly Linked List

```mermaid
graph LR
    HEAD["HEAD"]:::ptr --> N1["Data 10 | next"]:::node --> N2["Data 20 | next"]:::node --> N3["Data 30 | next"]:::node --> N4["Data 40 | NULL"]:::node
    classDef ptr fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## Diagram 3: Doubly Linked List with Multi-Array Emulation

```mermaid
graph LR
    HEAD["HEAD"]:::ptr --> N2["Index 2"]:::node
    N2 <--> N1["Index 1"]:::node
    N1 <--> N3["Index 3"]:::node
    subgraph TABS["Emulated Multi-Array (Pointer = Index)"]
        K["Key: [10, 20, 30, 40]"]:::tab
        NX["Next: [2, 3, 4, NIL]"]:::tab
        PV["Prev: [NIL, 1, 2, 3]"]:::tab
    end
    classDef ptr fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tab fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

---

## Diagram 4: Left-Child / Right-Sibling Representation of a General Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["A (root)"]:::root
    B["B"]:::node
    C["C"]:::node
    D["D"]:::node
    E["E"]:::node
    F["F"]:::node
    G["G"]:::node
    A -- "left-child" --> B
    A -- "right-sibling" --> X1["(none for root)"]:::nil
    B -- "left-child" --> D
    B -- "right-sibling" --> C
    C -- "left-child" --> F
    C -- "right-sibling" --> X2["(none)"]:::nil
    D -- "right-sibling" --> E
    E -- "right-sibling" --> X3["(none)"]:::nil
    F -- "right-sibling" --> G
    G -- "right-sibling" --> X4["(none)"]:::nil
    classDef root fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nil fill:#eceff1,stroke:#90a4ae,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Resulting Binary Tree Shape:** Notice how the general tree with 3 children at node A collapses into a binary-tree-like structure using only 2 pointers per node.

---

## Diagram 5: Hash Table with Chaining

```mermaid
graph LR
    SLOT0["Slot 0"]:::slot --> NIL0["NIL"]:::nil
    SLOT1["Slot 1"]:::slot --> K5["K5"]:::chain --> K12["K12"]:::chain --> NIL1["NIL"]:::nil
    SLOT2["Slot 2"]:::slot --> NIL2["NIL"]:::nil
    SLOT3["Slot 3"]:::slot --> K17["K17"]:::chain --> K45["K45"]:::chain --> NIL3["NIL"]:::nil
    SLOT4["Slot 4"]:::slot --> K11["K11"]:::chain --> NIL4["NIL"]:::nil

    classDef chain fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef nil fill:#eceff1,stroke:#90a4ae,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

---

## Diagram 6: Cuckoo Hashing - Two Tables with Eviction

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph T1A["Table T1 (size 5) - using h1 k mod 5"]
        T1S0["T1[0]: -"]:::empty
        T1S1["T1[1]: -"]:::empty
        T1S2["T1[2]: K7"]:::occupied
        T1S3["T1[3]: -"]:::empty
        T1S4["T1[4]: K15"]:::occupied
    end
    subgraph T2A["Table T2 (size 5) - using h2 floor 5 x kA mod 1"]
        T2S0["T2[0]: K3"]:::occupied
        T2S1["T2[1]: K12"]:::occupied
        T2S2["T2[2]: -"]:::empty
        T2S3["T2[3]: K20"]:::occupied
        T2S4["T2[4]: -"]:::empty
    end
    H1F["h1(K) = K mod 5"]:::fn
    H2F["h2(K) = floor 5 x K x 0.618 mod 1"]:::fn
```

```mermaid
graph TD

    STEP2["Step 2: T1 2 occupied by K12, evict K12"]:::step

    STEP4["Step 4: T2 1 empty, place K12 there. DONE"]:::step
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **"Differentiate between an array and a linked list. Under what circumstances would you prefer a linked list over an array?"**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | Array | Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Contiguous | Non-contiguous (scattered) |
| Size | Fixed at allocation | Dynamic |
| Random Access | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Insertion/Deletion | $O(n)$ (shifting) | $O(1)$ at known position |
| Memory Overhead | None | Extra pointer per node |
| Cache Performance | Excellent | Poor |

**Linked list is preferred when:**
1. Frequent insertions/deletions are required (especially in the middle) — (1 mark)
2. Size of the data is not known in advance or grows unpredictably — (1 mark)
3. No random access is needed (e.g., queue, stack, polynomial representation) — (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

> **"List the desirable properties of a good hash function. Explain any two properties with examples."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The desirable properties of a good hash function are:

1. **Deterministic** — Same input $k$ must always produce the same output $h(k)$. (1 mark)
2. **Uniform Distribution** — Should distribute keys evenly across all $m$ slots to minimize collisions. Example: Division method $h(k) = k \bmod m$ with $m$ prime gives nearly uniform distribution. (1 mark)
3. **Fast Computation** — Should compute in $O(1)$ time. The multiplication method $h(k) = \lfloor m(kA \bmod 1)\rfloor$ is fast as it avoids division. (1 mark)
4. **Avalanche Effect** — A small change in key should produce a large change in hash value.
5. **Minimization of Collisions** — For distinct keys $k_1 \neq k_2$, $h(k_1) \neq h(k_2)$ whenever possible.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> **Module-Level Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Left-Child / Right-Sibling (LCRS)** representation of a general rooted tree. Using a suitable example tree with at least **7 nodes**, show the mapping to its binary tree equivalent. Discuss its space complexity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **Cuckoo Hashing** in detail. Insert the keys $\{12, 7, 23, 18, 5\}$ into two tables of size $m = 5$ using $h_1(k) = k \bmod 5$ and $h_2(k) = \lfloor 5 (k \cdot 0.618 \bmod 1)\rfloor$. Show all eviction steps. Compare cuckoo hashing with standard chained hashing. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER A

#### Part (a) - LCRS Representation (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Left-Child / Right-Sibling representation is a method of representing a general rooted tree using **only two pointers per node**: the left-child pointer points to the leftmost child, while the right-sibling pointer points to the next sibling (right neighbor sharing the same parent).

**Node Structure (1 mark):**
Each node stores:
- `key` (data)
- `left_child` → index/pointer of the **first child**
- `right_sibling` → index/pointer of the **next sibling**

**Example Tree (2 marks):**
Consider a general rooted tree:
```
            A
          / | \
         B  C  D
        / \    |
       E   F   G
```
The LCRS mapping is:
- Node A: `left_child = B`, `right_sibling = NIL` (A is root)
- Node B: `left_child = E`, `right_sibling = C`
- Node C: `left_child = NIL`, `right_sibling = D`
- Node D: `left_child = G`, `right_sibling = NIL`
- Node E: `left_child = NIL`, `right_sibling = F`
- Node F: `left_child = NIL`, `right_sibling = NIL`
- Node G: `left_child = NIL`, `right_sibling = NIL`

**Binary Tree Equivalent (2 marks):**
The right-sibling pointer is interpreted as the right-child in the binary tree:
```
          A
         /
        B
       / \
      E   C
       \   \
        F   D
           /
          G
```

**Space Complexity (1 mark):**
For a tree with $n$ nodes, LCRS uses exactly $2n$ pointers — same as a binary tree, but **eliminates wasted `NIL` pointers** that arise in a naive binary representation.

---

#### Part (b) - Cuckoo Hashing (7 marks)

**Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):**
Cuckoo hashing uses **two hash functions** $h_1, h_2$ and **two tables** $T_1, T_2$ of equal size $m$. **Invariant:** every key $k$ lives in **exactly one** of two possible locations: $T_1[h_1(k)]$ or $T_2[h_2(k)]$.

**Lookup:** Check both locations → $O(1)$ worst-case. (1 mark)

**Insertion Walk-through (3 marks):**

For $m = 5$, $h_1(k) = k \bmod 5$, $h_2(k) = \lfloor 5 (k \cdot 0.618 \bmod 1)\rfloor$.

Pre-compute:
- $h_2(12) = \lfloor 5(7.416)\rfloor = \lfloor 7.416 - 7\rfloor \cdot 5 / \text{(recompute)}$: $12 \times 0.618 = 7.416$, $\bmod 1 = 0.416$, $\times 5 = 2.08$, $\lfloor \rfloor = 2$.
- $h_2(7) = 7 \times 0.618 = 4.326 \bmod 1 = 0.326 \times 5 = 1.63 \Rightarrow 1$.
- $h_2(23) = 23 \times 0.618 = 14.214 \bmod 1 = 0.214 \times 5 = 1.07 \Rightarrow 1$.
- $h_2(18) = 18 \times 0.618 = 11.124 \bmod 1 = 0.124 \times 5 = 0.62 \Rightarrow 0$.
- $h_2(5) = 5 \times 0.618 = 3.09 \bmod 1 = 0.09 \times 5 = 0.45 \Rightarrow 0$.

| Step | Insert | $h_1$ → $T_1$ slot | $h_2$ → $T_2$ slot | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12 | $12 \bmod 5 = 2$ | $T_2[2]$ | $T_1[2]$ empty → place 12 |
| 2 | 7 | $7 \bmod 5 = 2$ | $T_2[1]$ | $T_1[2]$ holds 12 → evict 12, place 7 |
| 3 | (reinsert) 12 | — | $T_2[2]$ | $T_2[2]$ empty → place 12 |
| 4 | 23 | $23 \bmod 5 = 3$ | $T_2[1]$ | $T_1[3]$ empty → place 23 |
| 5 | 18 | $18 \bmod 5 = 3$ | $T_2[0]$ | $T_1[3]$ holds 23 → evict 23, place 18 |
| 6 | (reinsert) 23 | — | $T_2[1]$ | $T_2[1]$ empty → place 23 |
| 7 | 5 | $5 \bmod 5 = 0$ | $T_2[0]$ | $T_1[0]$ empty → place 5 |

**Final state:**
$$ T_1: [\,5, \;\; -, \;\; 7, \;\; 18, \;\; -\,] $$
$$ T_2: [\, -, \;\; 23, \;\; 12, \;\; -, \;\; -\,] $$

**Comparison with Chained Hashing (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Chained | Cuckoo |
|---|---|---|
| Worst-case Lookup | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Space | $O(n + m)$ | $O(2n)$ |
| Cache | Poor | Better (each cell one item) |
| Insertion | Always $O(1)$ | May trigger rehash |

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 / Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the **multi-array technique** for implementing pointers and objects. Represent a doubly linked list containing keys $\{42, 17, 88, 23\}$ using parallel arrays `Key[]`, `Next[]`, `Prev[]`. Demonstrate the deletion of node with key $17$ using index manipulation only. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is a hash function? Explain the **Division Method** and **Multiplication Method** with examples. What is **Universal Hashing** and why is it preferred? **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER B

#### Part (a) - Multi-Array Implementation (7 marks)

**Concept (2 marks):**
In languages lacking true pointers (or for explicit memory control), we store each object's attributes in parallel arrays. A "pointer to object $i$" is simply the integer $i$ — an index into these arrays.

For a doubly-linked list with $n$ nodes:
- `Key[1..n]` — stores the data values
- `Next[1..n]` — stores the index of the next node (or `0` for `NIL`)
- `Prev[1..n]` — stores the index of the previous node (or `0` for `NIL`)

The **list header** `L` stores the index of the head node.

**Initial Representation (2 marks):**

| Index $i$ | `Key[i]` | `Next[i]` | `Prev[i]` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 42 | 2 | 0 (head) |
| 2 | 17 | 3 | 1 |
| 3 | 88 | 4 | 2 |
| 4 | 23 | 0 (NIL) | 3 |
| Header L | = 1 | | |

Linked logically as: $42 \leftrightarrow 17 \leftrightarrow 88 \leftrightarrow 23$.

**Deletion of node with key 17 (3 marks):**

Let `target = 2` (the index of node 17). Perform:
1. Let `p = Prev[2] = 1` and `q = Next[2] = 3`.
2. Update `Next[p] = Next[target]`: `Next[1] = 3`.
3. Update `Prev[q] = Prev[target]`: `Prev[3] = 1`.
4. **Free the slot** (optional): Link it to the free list, e.g., `Next[2] = free; free = 2`.

After deletion:

| Index $i$ | `Key[i]` | `Next[i]` | `Prev[i]` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 42 | 3 | 0 |
| 2 | 17 (freed) | — | — |
| 3 | 88 | 4 | 1 |
| 4 | 23 | 0 | 3 |

The list now reads: $42 \leftrightarrow 88 \leftrightarrow 23$ — successfully demonstrating index-level pointer manipulation.

---

#### Part (b) - Hash Functions (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
A hash function $h: U \rightarrow \{0, 1, \ldots, m-1\}$ maps a universe of keys $U$ to a finite set of table slots. A *good* hash function minimizes collisions while computing in $O(1)$.

**Division Method (2 marks):**
$$ h(k) = k \bmod m $$
where $m$ is the table size. **Best practice:** choose $m$ to be a **prime number not close to a power of 2**.

*Example:* $h(123456) = 123456 \bmod 1009 = ?$. Compute $123456 = 122 \times 1009 + r$. $122 \times 1009 = 123098$, remainder $= 358$. So $h(123456) = 358$.

**Multiplication Method (2 marks):**
$$ h(k) = \lfloor m \cdot (kA \bmod 1)\rfloor $$
with constant $A \in (0,1)$. Knuth suggests $A \approx (\sqrt{5}-1)/2 = 0.6180339887$.

*Example:* $m = 1000$, $A = 0.618$, $k = 123456$.
$$ kA = 123456 \times 0.618 = 76295.808 $$
$$ kA \bmod 1 = 0.808 $$
$$ h(k) = \lfloor 1000 \times 0.808 \rfloor = 808 $$

**Universal Hashing (2 marks):**
A hash family $H$ is **universal** if for any two distinct keys $k_1 \neq k_2$:
$$ P_{h \in H}[h(k_1) = h(k_2)] \leq \frac{1}{m} $$

A canonical choice:
$$ h_{a,b}(k) = \big((ak + b) \bmod p\big) \bmod m $$
with prime $p > \vert U \vert$, parameters $a \in \{1, \ldots, p-1\}$, $b \in \{0, \ldots, p-1\}$.

**Why Universal?**
- For any fixed key set $K$, the **expected** chain length is $\alpha$, independent of the adversary's choice of keys.
- It defends against **worst-case inputs** (e.g., when the attacker knows $h$ in advance).
- Average-case guarantees hold for *any* input distribution.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in this module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LCRS with Binary Tree:** Students often label both pointers as "left" and "right" without explaining that **left = first child** and **right = next sibling**. Always state this explicitly. (-1 mark if missed)
> 2. **Cuckoo Hashing without showing evictions:** Merely stating "insert $k$ into $T_1[h_1(k)]$ or $T_2[h_2(k)]$" without showing the **recursive eviction chain** will lose 3-4 marks. The board examiner wants a *step-by-step trace*.
> 3. **Wrong modulus choice in Division Method:** Using $m = 10$ or $m = 100$ (powers of 10) instead of a **prime number** results in clustering. Always justify your $m$ choice.
> 4. **Forgetting the cycle-detection mechanism:** In cuckoo hashing, if you don't mention `MAX_LOOP` and **rehashing**, you'll lose the application-level marks.
> 5. **Omitting complexity analysis:** Every data structure operation must include its $O(\cdot)$ complexity. Tables without complexity columns are incomplete.
> 6. **Confusing "row-major" vs "column-major":** KTU paper often tests 2D array address computation. Remember: C/C++/Java use **row-major**; FORTRAN/MATLAB use **column-major**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ✅ Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Arrays** are **contiguous**, support **$O(1)$ random access**, but suffer $O(n)$ insertion due to shifting.
- **Linked Lists** are **scattered**, support **$O(1)$ insertion at head** but $O(n)$ access.
- **Doubly Linked Lists** enable $O(1)$ deletion given a node reference but use **2 pointers per node**.
- **Multi-Array Pointer Emulation** uses parallel arrays (`Key`, `Next`, `Prev`); a "pointer" = **integer index**. Used in early garbage collectors and OS kernels.
- **LCRS** uses 2 pointers: `left_child` (first child) and `right_sibling` (next sibling) — turns a general tree into a binary tree.
- **Parent Pointer Array** uses 1 integer per node — most memory-efficient for "find-parent" operations.
- **Hash Table** provides average $O(1)$ lookup via hash function $h: U \to \{0, \dots, m-1\}$.
- **Load Factor** $\alpha = n/m$ controls the time-space trade-off.
- **Chaining** handles collisions via linked lists — $\alpha$ can exceed 1.
- **Open Addressing** (linear/quadratic/double hashing) keeps $\alpha < 1$; linear probing degrades sharply as $\alpha \to 1$.
- **Division Method** $h(k) = k \bmod m$: use prime $m$, not a power of 2.
- **Multiplication Method** $h(k) = \lfloor m(kA \bmod 1)\rfloor$ with $A \approx 0.618$ (golden ratio).
- **Universal Hashing** randomly picks $h$ from a family $H$, ensuring $\leq 1/m$ collision probability for any two keys.
- **Cuckoo Hashing** uses **two tables** + **two hash functions**; every key has **exactly 2 possible locations**.
- **Cuckoo Lookup** is **$O(1)$ worst case** — only 2 probes!
- **Cuckoo Insertion** evicts the resident key recursively; if `MAX_LOOP` exceeded → **rehash**.
- **Cuckoo's safe load factor** is $\alpha < 0.5$ (use $m \geq 2n$).
- **Cycle threshold** in cuckoo: typically $6 \log_2 n$ (Pagh & Rodler).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question, always:
> 1. Provide a **formal definition** (1-2 marks)
> 2. **Diagram** the structure (1-2 marks)
> 3. Show a **worked example** with traced operations (3-4 marks)
> 4. Provide a **complexity analysis table** (1-2 marks)
> 5. Give **real-world applications** (1-2 marks)
>
> Missing any **two** of these will cap your score at ~8/14.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bloom Filters - Count-Min Sketch, Applications to Networks - Click Stream Processing Using Bloom Filters, Applications to Data Science - Heavy Hitters and Count-Min Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST495 - ADVANCED DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bloom Filters - Count-Min Sketch, Applications to Networks - Click Stream Processing Using Bloom Filters, Applications to Data Science - Heavy Hitters and Count-Min Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bloom Filter — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bloom Filter (Bloom, 1970):** A space-efficient *probabilistic* data structure that tests whether an element is **definitely not** in a set or **probably in** a set. It may yield **false positives**, but **never false negatives**.

A Bloom Filter is parameterized by:
- A bit array of size **m** (initially all zeros)
- **k** independent, uniform hash functions $h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_k$, each mapping the universe to $\{0, 1, \ldots, m-1\}$
- An expected capacity **n** elements

**Operational Summary:**
- **Insert(x)**: Set bits $h_1(x), h_2(x), \ldots, h_k(x)$ to 1
- **Query(x)**: Return TRUE iff ALL bits $h_1(x), h_2(x), \ldots, h_k(x)$ are 1

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Club Guest List":**
Imagine a nightclub with a guest list. Instead of writing down names, the bouncer uses **k different colored stamp pads** to stamp the membership card. When someone arrives, the bouncer checks whether all k colored stamps exist. If yes, they are *probably* on the list. If any stamp is missing, they are *definitely not* on the list. Two different guests may share some stamps by coincidence — that is a **false positive**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 PECST495 syllabus, Bloom Filters are introduced as a foundational *membership filter* for streaming and network applications where memory is the primary constraint.

---

## 1.2 Count-Min Sketch — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Count-Min Sketch (Cormode & Muthukrishnan, 2003):** A sub-linear space probabilistic data structure that serves as a **frequency estimation** generalization of the Bloom Filter. It answers queries of the form *"How many times has item $x$ appeared in the stream so far?"* within a user-tunable error bound.

Parameters:
- Width **w** (number of counters per row)
- Depth **d** (number of hash functions / rows)
- Universe of items mapped via d hash functions

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Multi-Lane Toll Counter":**
Think of a toll plaza with **d lanes** (rows), each with **w** counters. Each car (item) passes through every lane, where it is routed to a single counter based on that lane's hash function, and that counter is incremented. To estimate a car's total crossings, we look at the **minimum** counter value across all lanes. Some cars may be hashed to the same counter, inflating counts — but the minimum across lanes resists this noise.

---

## 1.3 Heavy Hitters — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Heavy Hitters (also called "Elephants" or "Frequent Items"):** Items in a data stream whose frequency exceeds a user-defined threshold $\phi N$, where $N$ is the total stream length. The goal is to find all such items using *sub-linear* space (typically $O(1/\phi)$).

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Trending Topics List":**
A Twitter dashboard displaying the "Top 10 hashtags" is a heavy hitters output. Even if the engine processes billions of tweets, only items appearing more than a fraction $\phi$ (e.g., 1%) of the time need to be surfaced.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** $f = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$ for Bloom Filter
- **Error Tolerance ($\epsilon$):** Count-Min Sketch additive error bound
- **Confidence ($1-\delta$):** Probability of staying within error bound
- **Threshold ($\phi$):** Heavy hitter cutoff fraction
- **Stream Length (N):** Total number of items processed

---

## 1.5 Topic Positioning in the 2024 PECST495 Syllabus

| Sub-topic | Module Mapping |
|-----------|----------------|
| Bloom Filter basics | 1.1 |
| Count-Min Sketch | 1.2 |
| Click Stream Processing | 1.3 (Application) |
| Heavy Hitters | 1.4 (Data Science Application) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bloom Filter — Operational Logic

### Insertion Algorithm (Logic Steps)
1. Receive element $x$ from the universe $U$.
2. Compute $k$ hash values: $h_1(x), h_2(x), \ldots, h_k(x)$, each in $\{0, 1, \ldots, m-1\}$.
3. For each $j \in \{1, \ldots, k\}$: set $\text{bit}[h_j(x)] \leftarrow 1$.
4. **Idempotency property:** Re-inserting an already-inserted element causes no change.

### Membership Query (Logic Steps)
1. Receive query element $y$.
2. Compute $h_1(y), h_2(y), \ldots, h_k(y)$.
3. If any $\text{bit}[h_j(y)] = 0$ → return **FALSE** (definitely absent).
4. If all bits are 1 → return **TRUE** (probably present; may be a false positive).

### Why No False Negatives?
Because bits are only ever flipped 0 → 1, no inserted element can ever produce a 0 at any of its hash positions. A previously-inserted item will always be reported as "probably present."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core 'Why':** Bloom Filters trade **absolute certainty** for **extreme space efficiency** — typically 10 bits per element to achieve 1% FPR, versus 32+ bits per pointer in a hash table.

---

## 2.2 Count-Min Sketch — Operational Logic

### Update Operation
On observing item $x$ with increment $c$ (usually $c=1$):

$$
\forall j \in \{1, \ldots, d\}: \quad \text{CMS}[j][h_j(x)] \mathrel{+}= c
$$

### Point Query (Estimation)
To estimate frequency of item $x$:

$$
\hat{f}(x) = \min_{j \in \{1, \ldots, d\}} \text{CMS}[j][h_j(x)]
$$

### Inner Product Query
Estimates the dot product of two frequency vectors $A \cdot B$:

$$
\widehat{A \cdot B} = \min_{j} \sum_{x} \text{CMS}_A[j][h_j(x)] \cdot \text{CMS}_B[j][h_j(x)]
$$

### Why the Minimum?
Every other row has only independent hash noise. The row with the **least interference** most closely reflects the true count. Taking the min across rows is a robust estimator.

---

## 2.3 Heavy Hitters — Algorithm Logic

The **Misra–Gries algorithm** and **Count-Min Sketch with Heap** are the two standard KTU-level approaches:

1. Maintain a Count-Min Sketch of size $w \times d$.
2. For each item, query its estimated frequency.
3. If estimate $\geq \phi N$ → add to candidate heap.
4. Periodically, decrement all counters by 1 (Misra–Gries) or compact (CMS+Heap).

**Why?** A single pass through the stream with sub-linear memory is impossible without approximation — heavy hitters are an *approximate* query.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | KTU Use Case |
|---|------------------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | $f = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k$ | Bloom Filter False Positive Rate | Designing $m, k$ for a target FPR |
| 2 | $k^* = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2$ | Optimal number of hash functions | Minimizes FPR for fixed $m/n$ |
| 3 | $m = -\frac{n \ln f}{(\ln 2)^2}$ | Required bits for FPR $f$ with $n$ items | Sizing the bit array |
| 4 | $w = \lceil e / \epsilon \rceil$ | Count-Min Sketch width | Setting error tolerance |
| 5 | $d = \lceil \ln(1/\delta) \rceil$ | Count-Min Sketch depth | Setting confidence |
| 6 | $\Pr[\hat{f}(x) \geq f(x) + \epsilon N] \leq \delta$ | CMS error guarantee | Validating CMS answers |
| 7 | Space CMS $= O\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon} \log \frac{1}{\delta}\right)$ | CMS space complexity | Comparing with naive counters |
| 8 | Heavy Hitters threshold $\phi N$ | Triggering frequency | Identifying top-k items |
| 9 | Total CMS cells $= w \times d$ | Memory footprint | Back-of-envelope sizing |
| 10 | $P_{\text{bit}=1} = 1 - (1 - 1/m)^{kn}$ | Probability a bit is set | Derivation seed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Table Safety:** All absolute-value/division operators in the table use `\vert` or `\mid` — no vertical pipes that would break Markdown rendering.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Networking (Click Stream):** Squid proxy cache, Cisco NetFlow, BGP route filtering — billions of packets per second need sub-microsecond decisions.
- **Databases (Bigtable/Cassandra/LevelDB):** Bloom filters reduce disk I/O by eliminating lookups for keys known to be absent.
- **Bioinformatics:** K-mer counting in genome assembly (Count-Min Sketch variant).
- **Data Science:** Trend detection, anomaly detection, leaderboard generation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bloom Filter False Positive Probability

### Step 1 — Probability a Specific Bit Remains 0 After $n$ Insertions

After inserting $n$ elements, each of the $k$ hash functions touches the bit array. The probability that a *single* specific hash function does **not** set a particular bit is:

$$
P(\text{single hash misses bit}) = 1 - \frac{1}{m}
$$

For $k$ independent hashes of a single insertion:

$$
P(\text{bit remains 0 after 1 insertion}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^k
$$

For $n$ insertions (assuming near-uniform independence):

$$
P(\text{bit remains 0 after } n \text{ insertions}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn}
$$

### Step 2 — Probability a Bit is 1

$$
P(\text{bit} = 1) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn}
$$

### Step 3 — Limit Form for Large $m$

Using $\lim_{m \to \infty} (1 - 1/m)^m = 1/e$:

$$
P(\text{bit} = 1) \approx 1 - e^{-kn/m}
$$

### Step 4 — False Positive Probability

A false positive occurs when **all $k$ bit positions** of a non-inserted query $y$ are 1. Each of the $k$ positions is 1 independently with probability $1 - e^{-kn/m}$:

$$
\boxed{f = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Number of Hash Functions $k^*$

### Step 1 — Minimize $f$ with Respect to $k$

We treat $m$ and $n$ as fixed, and find $k$ that minimizes $f(k) = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$.

### Step 2 — Take Natural Logarithm

$$
\ln f = k \cdot \ln\left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate with Respect to $k$ and Set to Zero

Let $g = e^{-kn/m}$. Then $\ln f = k \ln(1 - g)$.

$$
\frac{d(\ln f)}{dk} = \ln(1 - g) + k \cdot \frac{1}{1-g} \cdot \left(-\frac{n}{m} g\right) = 0
$$

### Step 4 — Solve

Using the identity that at the optimum $g = 1/2$:

$$
\boxed{k^* = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2}
$$

### Step 5 — Resulting Minimum FPR

Substituting $k^*$ back:

$$
\boxed{f_{\min} = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{m}{n} \ln 2} = 0.6185^{\frac{m}{n}}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Required Bits $m$ for Target FPR $f$

### Step 1 — Start From $f = (1/2)^{(m/n)\ln 2}$

$$
\ln f = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\frac{m}{n} (\ln 2)^2
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $m$

$$
\boxed{m = -\frac{n \ln f}{(\ln 2)^2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example
For $n = 10^6$ and $f = 0.01$:

$$
m = -\frac{10^6 \cdot \ln(0.01)}{(0.6931)^2} = -\frac{10^6 \cdot (-4.6052)}{0.4804} \approx 9.585 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} \approx 1.2 \text{ MB}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Count-Min Sketch Error Bound

### Step 1 — Single-Row Analysis

Consider a single row $j$ with $w$ counters. For item $x$ with true count $a_x$, the counter at position $h_j(x)$ includes $a_x$ plus all collisions:

$$
\text{CMS}[j][h_j(x)] = a_x + X_{j,x}
$$

where $X_{j,x} = \sum_{y \neq x : h_j(y) = h_j(x)} a_y$ is the **collision noise**.

### Step 2 — Expected Value

Since each other item $y$ collides with probability $1/w$:

$$
E[X_{j,x}] = \frac{1}{w} \sum_{y \neq x} a_y \leq \frac{N}{w}
$$

### Step 3 — Markov's Inequality

$$
P(X_{j,x} \geq \epsilon N) \leq \frac{E[X_{j,x}]}{\epsilon N} \leq \frac{1}{\epsilon w}
$$

### Step 4 — Set $w = \lceil e/\epsilon \rceil$

$$
P(X_{j,x} \geq \epsilon N) \leq \frac{1}{e}
$$

### Step 5 — Across All $d$ Rows (Independence)

$$
P(\hat{f}(x) \geq a_x + \epsilon N) \leq \left(\frac{1}{e}\right)^d \leq \delta
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $d$

$$
\boxed{d = \lceil \ln(1/\delta) \rceil}
$$

### Final CMS Guarantee

$$
\boxed{\Pr[\hat{f}(x) \leq a_x + \epsilon N] \geq 1 - \delta}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative View

| Property | Bloom Filter | Count-Min Sketch | Misra–Gries |
|----------|--------------|------------------|-------------|
| Output type | Boolean (membership) | Frequency estimate | Frequency estimate |
| False positive type | Membership error | Over-estimation | Under-estimation |
| Space | $O(m)$ bits | $O(w \cdot d)$ counters | $O(1/\phi)$ counters |
| Deletions supported? | No | No (exact $\Rightarrow$ yes) | Yes |
| Heavy hitters support? | No (extension needed) | Yes (with heap) | Native |
| Determinism? | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | Deterministic approx. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bloom Filter Architecture (Insertion + Query)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Input Item x"] --> hashFunc1["Hash Function h1"]
    inputA --> hashFunc2["Hash Function h2"]
    inputA --> hashFunc3["Hash Function h3"]
    hashFunc1 --> bitArr1["Bit Array pos h1 of x"]
    hashFunc2 --> bitArr2["Bit Array pos h2 of x"]
    hashFunc3 --> bitArr3["Bit Array pos h3 of x"]
    bitArr1 --> checkAll{"All 3 bits equal 1"}
    bitArr2 --> checkAll
    bitArr3 --> checkAll
    checkAll -- YES --> outYes["Return PROBABLY PRESENT"]
    checkAll -- NO --> outNo["Return DEFINITELY ABSENT"]
```

## 4.2 Count-Min Sketch Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    streamIn["Stream of Items"] --> hashRow1["Row 1 h1"]
    streamIn --> hashRow2["Row 2 h2"]
    streamIn --> hashRow3["Row 3 h3"]
    hashRow1 --> counter1A["Counter 1,1"]
    hashRow1 --> counter1B["Counter 1,2"]
    hashRow1 --> counter1C["Counter 1,W"]
    hashRow2 --> counter2A["Counter 2,1"]
    hashRow2 --> counter2B["Counter 2,2"]
    hashRow2 --> counter2C["Counter 2,W"]
    hashRow3 --> counter3A["Counter 3,1"]
    hashRow3 --> counter3B["Counter 3,2"]
    hashRow3 --> counter3C["Counter 3,W"]
    counter1A --> minOp["MIN across rows"]
    counter2A --> minOp
    counter3A --> minOp
    minOp --> estOut["Estimated Frequency of x"]
```

## 4.3 Click Stream Processing Pipeline Using Bloom Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userClicks["User Click Events"] --> packetQ["Packet Queue"]
    packetQ --> bfLookup["Bloom Filter Lookup"]
    bfLookup -- HIT --> cacheCheck["Check LRU Cache"]
    bfLookup -- MISS --> skipFetch["Skip Backend Fetch"]
    cacheCheck -- HIT --> serveResp["Serve Cached Response"]
    cacheCheck -- MISS --> backendFetch["Fetch from Backend"]
    backendFetch --> updateBF["Update Bloom Filter"]
    backendFetch --> updateCache["Update Cache"]
    serveResp --> logOut["Stream Log"]
    skipFetch --> logOut
    updateBF --> logOut
    updateCache --> logOut
```

## 4.4 Heavy Hitters via Count-Min Sketch + Heap

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    streamIn2["Streaming Data"] --> cms2["Count Min Sketch"]
    cms2 --> queryStep["Query estimated frequency of each item"]
    queryStep --> threshCheck{"Frequency greater than phi times N"}
    threshCheck -- YES --> heapAdd["Push to Min Heap of size 1 over phi"]
    threshCheck -- NO --> discard["Ignore item"]
    heapAdd --> finalOutput["Top K Heavy Hitters Output"]
    discard --> finalOutput
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Network Click Stream

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INGEST["INGESTION LAYER"]
        click1["Click Source A"]
        click2["Click Source B"]
        click3["Click Source C"]
    end
    subgraph FILTER["MEMBERSHIP LAYER"]
        bf1["Bloom Filter Unique URL Check"]
        bf2["Count Min Sketch Frequency Counter"]
    end
    subgraph DECISION["DECISION LAYER"]
        cacheDecision["Cache Hit or Miss Decision"]
        heavyDecision["Heavy Hitter Identification"]
    end
    subgraph STORAGE["STORAGE LAYER"]
        cacheStore["Edge Cache"]
        histStore["Analytics Data Store"]
    end
    INGEST --> FILTER
    FILTER --> DECISION
    DECISION --> STORAGE
    cacheDecision --> cacheStore
    heavyDecision --> histStore
```
