2. Suppose a `DECREASE-KEY` triggers a `CUT` on $a$. Now $a$ is a root, and `CUT` sets $a.mark = FALSE$. The parent $p$ is notified via `CASCADING-CUT(p)`. Since $p.mark = FALSE$, the cascade simply sets $p.mark = TRUE$ and stops.
3. Now another `DECREASE-KEY` triggers a `CUT` on $b$. Again $b$ becomes a root, $p.mark = TRUE$, so `CASCADING-CUT` does:
   - `CUT(H, p, p.parent)` — $p$ itself is cut and becomes a root.
   - `CASCADING-CUT(H, p.parent)` — recursion continues upward.

**Purpose:** Marks limit the number of cascading cuts. A node can lose at most one child *per cascade* before being cut itself, guaranteeing the amortized $O(1)$ bound on `DECREASE-KEY`.

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Theorem (Degree Bound):** For a Fibonacci Heap $H$ with $n$ nodes, $D(n) \le \lfloor \log_{\varphi} n \rfloor$.

**Proof Outline (faithful to the textbook Cormen et al. derivation):**

**Lemma 1 (Subtree Size):** If $x$ is any node in $H$ with $d(x) = k$, then $\text{size}(x) \ge F_{k+2}$, where $F_j$ is the $j$-th Fibonacci number with $F_0 = 0, F_1 = 1$.

*Proof sketch:* Children $c_1(x), c_2(x), \dots, c_k(x)$ are added to $x$ in that order. When $c_i$ was linked, it already had at least $i-1$ children of its own, so its *current* degree was $\ge i-1$. By the structure, $c_i$ can lose at most one child (via a cascading cut) before being cut itself. Thus $s(c_i) \ge s(c_{i+1}) + 1$. Summing the geometric-like series recovers the Fibonacci bound.

**Lemma 2 (Binet's Formula):** $F_{k+2} \ge \varphi^{k}$ where $\varphi = (1+\sqrt{5})/2$.

*Proof sketch:* $F_{k+2} = \dfrac{\varphi^{k+2} - \psi^{k+2}}{\sqrt{5}}$ with $\psi = (1-\sqrt{5})/2 \approx -0.618$, so $\vert \psi^{k+2} \vert < 1$ and $F_{k+2} \ge \dfrac{\varphi^{k+2} - 1}{\sqrt{5}} \ge \varphi^{k}$ for all $k \ge 0$.

**Main Proof:**

Let $x$ be a node of maximum degree $D(n)$ in an $n$-node heap. Then:

$$
n \;\ge\; \text{size}(x) \;\ge\; F_{D(n)+2} \;\ge\; \varphi^{D(n)} \quad \text{(by Lemmas 1 and 2)}
$$

Taking $\log_{\varphi}$ of both sides:

$$
D(n) \;\le\; \log_{\varphi} n
$$

Since $D(n)$ is an integer:

$$
\boxed{\, D(n) \;\le\; \lfloor \log_{\varphi} n \rfloor \,}
$$

Equivalently, $D(n) \le \lfloor 1.4404 \log_2 n \rfloor$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Fibonacci Heaps**

- **Definition:** Lazy collection of min-heap-ordered trees with a circular doubly linked root list, accessed via `H.min`.
- **Node Fields:** `key`, `parent`, `child`, `left`, `right`, `degree`, `mark`.
- **Six Operations & Amortized Costs:** MAKE-HEAP $O(1)$, INSERT $O(1)$, MINIMUM $O(1)$, EXTRACT-MIN $O(\log n)$, DECREASE-KEY $O(1)$, UNION $O(1)$.
- **Internal Subroutines:** `LINK(y, x)`, `CONSOLIDATE(H)`, `CUT(H, x, y)`, `CASCADING-CUT(H, y)`.
- **Potential Function:** $\Phi(H) = t(H) + 2m(H)$. Always non-negative, zero for empty heap.
- **Golden Ratio:** $\varphi = (1+\sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618$. Maximum degree bound: $D(n) \le \lfloor \log_{\varphi} n \rfloor$.
- **Why "Fibonacci"?** Subtree sizes grow at least as $F_{k+2}$, hence the name.
- **Mark Flag Purpose:** Prevents any one node from being the cut victim more than once before it is itself cut, limiting cascading cuts to $O(1)$ amortized.
- **Time-Space Tradeoff:** Fibonacci Heaps trade a slightly higher **constant factor** in practice for theoretically superior asymptotics — they are *rarely* faster than Binary Heaps for $n < 1000$.
- **Used By:** Dijkstra's shortest path, Prim's MST (asymptotically optimal variants).
- **Exam Mantra:** *"Insert, Decrease-Key, Union — constant amortized. Extract-Min — logarithmic amortized. Everything else — worst-case constant."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra (Repeat 3 times before the exam):**
> *"Fibonacci Heap = Lazy + Mark + Cascading-Cut + Golden Ratio."*
> Every operation, every proof, every trace flows from these four pillars. If you remember them, you can reconstruct any answer even under exam stress.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Merkle Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST495 - ADVANCED DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Merkle Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Merkle Tree** (also known as a **Hash Tree**) is a binary (or generalized $n$-ary) tree data structure in which every **leaf node** is labelled with the cryptographic hash of a data block, and every **non-leaf node** is labelled with the cryptographic hash of the labels of its child nodes. The single top-most node, called the **Merkle Root**, serves as a compact, tamper-evident fingerprint of the entire underlying data set.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Merkle Tree is a hierarchical hash-based data structure that enables efficient and secure verification of large data structures. It allows the contents of a data set to be verified in $O(\log n)$ time and space, where $n$ is the number of data blocks.

The general recursive structure is given by:

$$H_{\text{node}} = \text{Hash}(H_{\text{left}} \Vert H_{\text{right}})$$

where $\Vert$ denotes **bit-string concatenation** and $\text{Hash}(\cdot)$ is a cryptographic hash function such as **SHA-256** (standard in Bitcoin), **Keccak-256** (used in Ethereum), or **BLAKE2**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology to Master for KTU Exams:**
> - **Leaf Node:** Contains the hash of an individual data block $D_i$.
> - **Internal Node:** Contains the hash of its children's concatenated hashes.
> - **Merkle Root:** The single root hash summarizing all data.
> - **Merkle Path / Proof of Inclusion:** The minimal sibling-hash chain required to recompute the root.
> - **Authentication Path:** Synonym for the Merkle Path.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Fingerprint Family Tree

Imagine a large family with millions of members, and you need to prove that a specific person "Rahul" belongs to this family, but showing the entire family database is impractical.

**How Merkle Trees solve this:**

1. Every individual family member is given a unique **fingerprint** (leaf hash).
2. Pairs of members are grouped, and a **superior officer computes a combined fingerprint** of the pair (internal node).
3. This pairing continues up the hierarchy until **one single "Chief Family Fingerprint"** exists at the top — the Merkle Root.

If someone asks, *"Is Rahul part of this family?"*, you don't show the entire database. You only provide:
- Rahul's fingerprint
- The sibling fingerprints along the path from Rahul's leaf to the Chief Fingerprint

The verifier can recompute the Chief Fingerprint and check if it matches the publicly known root. **This is the essence of a Merkle Proof.**

## 1.3 Real-World Significance

- **Bitcoin & Blockchain:** Each block's header contains a Merkle Root summarizing all transactions; Simplified Payment Verification (SPV) uses Merkle proofs.
- **Git Version Control:** Git uses a Merkle DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) of content-addressed objects.
- **IPFS (InterPlanetary File System):** Files are split into chunks and referenced via a Merkle DAG.
- **Certificate Transparency (CT) Logs:** Used by browsers (Chrome, Safari) to publicly log all SSL/TLS certificates.
- **Distributed Databases (Cassandra, DynamoDB):** Anti-entropy synchronization uses Merkle trees to detect inconsistencies between replicas.
- **NoSQL systems (Amazon Dynamo, Riak):** Used for fast divergence detection with $O(\log n)$ comparison complexity.

## 1.4 Cryptographic Hash Function — The Engine of Merkle Trees

A Merkle Tree is only as secure as its underlying hash function. The hash function $\text{Hash}: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^k$ must satisfy:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Mandatory Security Properties:**
> 1. **Pre-image Resistance:** Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find $m$ such that $\text{Hash}(m)=h$.
> 2. **Second Pre-image Resistance:** Given $m_1$, it is infeasible to find $m_2 \neq m_1$ with $\text{Hash}(m_1) = \text{Hash}(m_2)$.
> 3. **Collision Resistance:** It is infeasible to find any two distinct $m_1, m_2$ with $\text{Hash}(m_1) = \text{Hash}(m_2)$.

Standard output size $k = \mathbf{256\text{ bits}}$ (32 bytes) for **SHA-256**, giving a hash space of $2^{256}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Construction (Bottom-Up Algorithm)

The construction of a Merkle Tree follows a deterministic, bottom-up procedure. Given $n$ data blocks $\{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n\}$:

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 (Leaf Layer):** Compute the hash of each raw data block.
  $$L_i = \text{Hash}(D_i), \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$$

- **Step 2 (Pairing):** Group adjacent leaf nodes in pairs $(L_1, L_2), (L_3, L_4), \dots$

- **Step 3 (Internal Hashing):** For each pair, compute the parent node hash by concatenating the child hashes and re-hashing.
  $$P_j = \text{Hash}(L_{2j-1} \Vert L_{2j})$$

- **Step 4 (Iterate):** Treat the newly computed parent hashes as a new "leaf" layer and repeat Steps 2 and 3.

- **Step 5 (Termination):** Continue until exactly one hash remains at the top — the **Merkle Root** $R$.

- **Edge Case — Odd Number of Leaves:** If $n$ is odd at any level, the last unpaired hash is **duplicated** (promoted by self-pairing), or in some implementations, it is **promoted unchanged** to the next level.
  $$P_{\text{last}} = \text{Hash}(L_{\text{last}} \Vert L_{\text{last}})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters:** The bottom-up nature ensures that any tampering with a single leaf $D_i$ **propagates** to the root, completely changing every ancestor's hash and the final Merkle Root. This is the source of Merkle Trees' **tamper-evident** property.

## 2.2 Merkle Proof / Proof of Inclusion (Authentication Path)

A **Merkle Proof** for a data block $D_x$ is the ordered set of sibling hashes along the unique path from leaf $L_x$ to the root $R$.

**Verification Procedure:**
1. Verifier receives $D_x$ and the authentication path $\{(s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k)\}$ where $s_i$ is the sibling hash at level $i$.
2. Verifier computes $L_x = \text{Hash}(D_x)$.
3. Verifier iteratively reconstructs the path upward using $s_i$:
   $$h_1 = \text{Hash}(L_x \Vert s_1) \text{ or } \text{Hash}(s_1 \Vert L_x)$$
   (depending on whether $L_x$ is a left or right child)
4. Continue until the recomputed root $R'$ is obtained.
5. **Accept** if $R' = R_{\text{known}}$; **Reject** otherwise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "How" Matters:** The verifier never sees the full dataset. They only need $\log_2 n$ hashes and the root. This is what makes Merkle Trees the cornerstone of **light-client verification** in blockchain networks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Complexity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Leaf hash computation | $L_i = \text{Hash}(D_i)$ | One hash per data block |
| Internal node hash | $P_j = \text{Hash}(L_{2j-1} \Vert L_{2j})$ | Recursive parent construction |
| Total hash operations (build) | $T(n) = n - 1$ | For $n$ leaves, a binary Merkle tree computes $n-1$ internal hashes |
| Build time complexity | $O(n)$ | Linear in number of data blocks |
| **Proof size (Merkle path length)** | $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ | Number of sibling hashes in a proof |
| **Verification time complexity** | $O(\log n)$ | Recompute root in logarithmic steps |
| **Proof size (storage)** | $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil \times k$ bits | Where $k$ is hash output size (e.g., 256 bits for SHA-256) |
| **Total tree height** | $h = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ | For perfectly balanced binary Merkle tree |
| SHA-256 output size | $k = \mathbf{256\text{ bits}} = 32\text{ bytes}$ | Standard for Bitcoin, Git |
| Hash space | $2^{256} \approx 1.158 \times 10^{77}$ | Practically collision-free |
| Worst-case duplicates for odd $n$ | Up to $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ | Duplication depth |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked for the size of a Merkle proof, students often answer "$n$" or "the entire tree." The correct answer is **$\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ hashes** — this logarithmic efficiency is the entire point of the data structure.

## 2.4 Properties Summary

| Property | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Structure** | Binary tree (generalizable to $k$-ary) |
| **Deterministic** | Yes — same input always yields same root |
| **Tamper-evident** | Any single-bit change in any leaf changes the root |
| **Verifiable subset** | Yes — via $O(\log n)$ proof |
| **Merkle Root uniqueness** | Practically unique (collision resistance) |
| **Append-friendly** | No — re-balancing required for arbitrary inserts |
| **Sort-friendly variant** | Merkle Sorted Tree (used in Certificate Transparency) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Why Merkle Trees? |
|---|---|---|
| **Cryptocurrency** | Bitcoin block header | Compact proof of transaction inclusion |
| **Version Control** | Git object storage | Efficient diffing and integrity |
| **Cloud Storage** | AWS S3, Dropbox | Block-level deduplication with integrity |
| **Distributed DBs** | Cassandra anti-entropy | Fast replica divergence detection |
| **PKI** | Certificate Transparency | Public auditability of issued certificates |
| **Networking** | P2P file sharing (BitTorrent) | Piece-level verification |
| **AI/ML Pipelines** | Model versioning (DVC, MLflow) | Reproducibility and integrity of datasets |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Number of Hash Operations for Construction

**Given:** A balanced binary Merkle tree with $n$ leaf nodes (assume $n = 2^h$ for simplicity).

**Step 1:** At the leaf level, we have $n$ leaf hashes already computed (one per data block).

**Step 2:** At level 1 (one level above leaves), we have $n/2$ internal nodes. Each requires exactly 1 hash operation.

$$\text{Hashes at level 1} = \frac{n}{2}$$

**Step 3:** At level 2, we have $n/4$ internal nodes.

$$\text{Hashes at level 2} = \frac{n}{4}$$

**Step 4:** Continue until the root level (level $h$), which has exactly 1 hash operation.

**Step 5:** Sum the geometric series:

$$T(n) = \frac{n}{2} + \frac{n}{4} + \frac{n}{8} + \dots + \frac{n}{2^h} + 1$$

**Step 6:** Apply the geometric series formula $\sum_{i=1}^{h} \frac{n}{2^i} = n \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^h}\right)$:

$$T(n) = n \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^h}\right) = n - 1$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{T(n) = n - 1 \text{ hash operations}}$$

For $n = 2^h$, building a Merkle tree is **$O(n)$** in time.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Merkle Proof Size and Verification Complexity

**Given:** A Merkle tree with $n$ data blocks, height $h = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$.

**Step 1:** To verify that data block $D_x$ is part of the tree, we must traverse from leaf $L_x$ to the root.

**Step 2:** At each level, the verifier needs exactly **one sibling hash** (provided by the prover).

**Step 3:** Number of levels traversed:

$$\text{Path length} = h = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the proof consists of exactly $h$ sibling hashes:

$$\text{Proof size} = h \text{ hashes} = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil \text{ hashes}$$

**Step 5:** In bits (using SHA-256 with $k = 256$ bits):

$$\text{Proof size in bits} = 256 \cdot \lceil \log_2 n \rceil \text{ bits} = 32 \cdot \lceil \log_2 n \rceil \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 6:** Verification requires one hash computation per level:

$$\text{Verification operations} = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil = O(\log n)$$

**Numerical Example:** For $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ transactions (as in a typical Bitcoin block):

$$h = \lceil \log_2(10^6) \rceil = 20$$

$$\text{Proof size} = 20 \text{ SHA-256 hashes} = 640 \text{ bytes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters:** A million-transaction block can be proven to contain a specific transaction with only **640 bytes** of proof and **20 hash operations** — versus the multi-megabyte full block. This enables lightweight mobile wallets (SPV clients) to verify payments securely.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Storage Complexity

**Given:** $n$ data blocks and a hash output of $k$ bits.

**Step 1:** Storage for leaf hashes: $n \cdot k$ bits.

**Step 2:** Storage for internal hashes: $(n - 1) \cdot k$ bits (one per internal node, including root).

**Step 3:** Total Merkle tree storage (excluding raw data):

$$S_{\text{tree}} = (2n - 1) \cdot k \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4:** For $k = 256$ bits and $n$ blocks:

$$S_{\text{tree}} = 256(2n - 1) \text{ bits} = 32(2n - 1) \text{ bytes}$$

**Conclusion:** Storage is $O(n)$ in the number of blocks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Merkle Tree Variants

| Variant | Branching Factor $b$ | Tree Height $h$ | Proof Size | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Binary Merkle Tree** | 2 | $\log_2 n$ | $\log_2 n$ hashes | Bitcoin, generic |
| **Merkle Patricia Tree** | 16 (hex) | $\log_{16} n$ | $\log_{16} n$ nodes | Ethereum state |
| **$n$-ary Merkle Tree** | $k$ | $\log_k n$ | $\log_k n$ hashes | Optimized I/O |
| **Sparse Merkle Tree** | 2 | $\log_2 (2^{256})$ | 256 hashes | Stateless cryptography |
| **Merkle Mountain Range** | 2 | Multiple peaks | Append-only | Time-series data |

**Derivations for non-binary variant:**

For a $k$-ary tree with $n$ leaves:

$$h_k = \lceil \log_k n \rceil$$

$$T_k(n) = \frac{n - 1}{k - 1} \text{ internal nodes}$$

$$\text{Proof size} = h_k \text{ hashes}$$

**Verification complexity:**

$$\boxed{O(\log_k n) \text{ hash operations}}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** Given 8 data blocks $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_8$, construct the Merkle tree using SHA-256 (assume simplified 4-bit hash output for illustration).

**Step 1: Compute leaf hashes.**

$$L_1 = \text{Hash}(D_1) = a3f1, \quad L_2 = \text{Hash}(D_2) = 7b2c, \quad \dots$$

**Step 2: Level 1 (4 internal nodes).**

$$P_1 = \text{Hash}(L_1 \Vert L_2), \quad P_2 = \text{Hash}(L_3 \Vert L_4), \quad \dots$$

**Step 3: Level 2 (2 internal nodes).**

$$Q_1 = \text{Hash}(P_1 \Vert P_2), \quad Q_2 = \text{Hash}(P_3 \Vert P_4)$$

**Step 4: Root.**

$$R = \text{Hash}(Q_1 \Vert Q_2)$$

**Proof of Inclusion for $D_3$:**

$$\text{Path} = \{L_4, P_2, Q_2\}$$

**Verification cost:** $h = \log_2 8 = 3$ hashes, $3 \times 256 = 768$ bits $= 96$ bytes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Binary Merkle Tree — Full Structure (8-Block Example)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1["Merkle Root R = hash of Q1 and Q2"]

    q1Node["Q1 = hash of P1 and P2"]
    q2Node["Q2 = hash of P3 and P4"]

    p1Node["P1 = hash of L1 and L2"]
    p2Node["P2 = hash of L3 and L4"]
    p3Node["P3 = hash of L5 and L6"]
    p4Node["P4 = hash of L7 and L8"]

    l1Node["L1 = hash of D1"]
    l2Node["L2 = hash of D2"]
    l3Node["L3 = hash of D3"]
    l4Node["L4 = hash of D4"]
    l5Node["L5 = hash of D5"]
    l6Node["L6 = hash of D6"]
    l7Node["L7 = hash of D7"]
    l8Node["L8 = hash of D8"]

    root1 --> q1Node
    root1 --> q2Node
    q1Node --> p1Node
    q1Node --> p2Node
    q2Node --> p3Node
    q2Node --> p4Node
    p1Node --> l1Node
    p1Node --> l2Node
    p2Node --> l3Node
    p2Node --> l4Node
    p3Node --> l5Node
    p3Node --> l6Node
    p4Node --> l7Node
    p4Node --> l8Node
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Every internal node is computed by hashing the concatenation of its two child hashes. The arrows denote the parent-child derivation relationship in the bottom-up construction phase.

---

## 4.2 Merkle Proof Verification — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    proverA["Prover Side: Holds Full Dataset"]
    proverB["Target Data Block D3"]
    proverC["Authentication Path: L4, P2, Q2"]
    proverD["Known Merkle Root R published"]

    verifierA["Verifier Side: Light Client"]
    verifierB["Receives D3 and Path: L4, P2, Q2"]
    verifierC["Step 1: Compute L3 hash of D3"]
    verifierD["Step 2: Compute X1 hash of L3 concat L4"]
    verifierE["Step 3: Compute X2 hash of P2 concat X1"]
    verifierF["Step 4: Compute X3 hash of Q2 concat X2"]
    verifierG["Comparison: X3 equals R?"]
    verifierH["If equal: ACCEPT. Else: REJECT"]

    proverA --> proverB
    proverA --> proverC
    proverD --> verifierA
    verifierA --> verifierB
    verifierB --> verifierC
    verifierC --> verifierD
    verifierD --> verifierE
    verifierE --> verifierF
    verifierF --> verifierG
    verifierG --> verifierH
```

> **Process Flow Description:** The prover (e.g., a full Bitcoin node) provides the verifier (e.g., a mobile SPV wallet) with the target data block and the sibling-hash chain. The verifier recomputes the path upward and compares the resulting root against the publicly known root. Only $O(\log n)$ hashes are exchanged.

---

## 4.3 Construction Phase vs. Verification Phase Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    phaseA["Phase 1: Tree Construction (Offline, One-Time)"]
    phaseAa["Input: n Data Blocks D1 to Dn"]
    phaseAb["Hash every block to form n leaves"]
    phaseAc["Pair and hash upward iteratively"]
    phaseAd["Output: Merkle Root R stored publicly"]

    phaseB["Phase 2: Proof Verification (Online, Per-Query)"]
    phaseBa["Input: Target Block Dx and Authentication Path"]
    phaseBb["Recompute leaf hash of Dx"]
    phaseBc["Apply sibling hashes level by level"]
    phaseBd["Compare recomputed root with known R"]

    phaseA --> phaseAa
    phaseAa --> phaseAb
    phaseAb --> phaseAc
    phaseAc --> phaseAd
    phaseAd --> phaseB
    phaseB --> phaseBa
    phaseBa --> phaseBb
    phaseBb --> phaseBc
    phaseBc --> phaseBd
```

---

## 4.4 Tamper-Evidence Propagation Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    origA["Original D3 hash L3"]
    origB["Original P2 hash"]
    origC["Original Q1 hash"]
    origD["Original Merkle Root R"]

    tamperedA["Tampered D3 hash L3 PRIME (changed)"]
    tamperedB["Tampered P2 PRIME (changed)"]
    tamperedC["Tampered Q1 PRIME (changed)"]
    tamperedD["Tampered Merkle Root R PRIME (changed)"]

    origA -.unchanged.-> origB
    origB -.unchanged.-> origC
    origC -.unchanged.-> origD

    origA ==changed==> tamperedA
    tamperedA ==propagates==> tamperedB
    tamperedB ==propagates==> tamperedC
    tamperedC ==propagates==> tamperedD

    tamperedD -.verification FAILS.-> rejectNode["Verification Rejected: R PRIME not equal to published R"]
```

> **Critical Insight:** A single-bit change at any leaf cascades all the way to the root, changing $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ intermediate hashes. Verifier immediately detects this because the recomputed root will not match the published root.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Merkle Tree. List any two properties of the cryptographic hash function used in its construction.**

**Model Answer:**

A Merkle Tree is a binary tree data structure in which every leaf node contains the hash of a data block and every internal node contains the hash of the concatenated hashes of its children. The topmost node, called the Merkle Root, acts as a single fingerprint of the entire data set.

Two key properties of the cryptographic hash function are:

1. **Pre-image Resistance:** Given a hash value $h$, it is computationally infeasible to determine the original input $m$ such that $\text{Hash}(m) = h$.
2. **Collision Resistance:** It is computationally infeasible to find two distinct inputs $m_1 \neq m_2$ such that $\text{Hash}(m_1) = \text{Hash}(m_2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always relate the property back to the Merkle Tree's security. E.g., "Collision resistance ensures that two different data sets cannot produce the same Merkle Root."

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a Merkle Proof? Why is its size logarithmic in the number of data blocks?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Merkle Proof** (or authentication path) is the ordered sequence of sibling hashes along the unique path from a target leaf node to the Merkle Root. It allows a verifier to confirm that a specific data block belongs to a data set summarized by a known root hash, without downloading the entire data set.

The proof size is $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ hashes (i.e., $O(\log n)$) because:
- The Merkle tree is a binary tree of height $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$.
- To traverse from any leaf to the root, one sibling hash is required per level.
- Therefore, only $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ hashes are needed regardless of how large $n$ grows.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following. Both questions carry equal marks and test the same Course Outcome at the **Apply / Analyze** cognitive level.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Construct a Merkle Tree for the following four data blocks using SHA-256. Show all hash values (you may assume simplified 4-bit output for illustration).
Data blocks: $D_1 = \text{"KTU"}$, $D_2 = \text{"CSE"}$, $D_3 = \text{"ADS"}$, $D_4 = \text{"2024"}$

Assume:
- $\text{Hash}(\text{"KTU"}) = 1A2B$
- $\text{Hash}(\text{"CSE"}) = 3C4D$
- $\text{Hash}(\text{"ADS"}) = 5E6F$
- $\text{Hash}(\text{"2024"}) = 7890$

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute leaf hashes (Level 0).**

$$L_1 = \text{Hash}(D_1) = 1A2B$$
$$L_2 = \text{Hash}(D_2) = 3C4D$$
$$L_3 = \text{Hash}(D_3) = 5E6F$$
$$L_4 = \text{Hash}(D_4) = 7890$$

**Step 2: Compute internal hashes (Level 1).**

$$P_1 = \text{Hash}(L_1 \Vert L_2) = \text{Hash}(\text{"1A2B3C4D"}) = \text{BEEF}$$
$$P_2 = \text{Hash}(L_3 \Vert L_4) = \text{Hash}(\text{"5E6F7890"}) = \text{CAFE}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Merkle Root (Level 2).**

$$R = \text{Hash}(P_1 \Vert P_2) = \text{Hash}(\text{"BEEFCAFE"}) = \text{DEAD}$$

**Merkle Tree Diagram:**

```
            R = DEAD
           /       \
       P1=BEEF    P2=CAFE
       /   \      /    \
   L1=1A2B L2=3C4D L3=5E6F L4=7890
    [D1]   [D2]   [D3]    [D4]
```

---

**(b)** Using the tree constructed in part (a), provide the **Merkle Proof** for data block $D_3$ and explain step-by-step how a verifier would use it to confirm $D_3$'s inclusion. Calculate the proof size in bytes (assume SHA-256 output). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the authentication path for $D_3$.**

Starting from leaf $L_3$ and moving up to root $R$:
- Sibling of $L_3$ at Level 0: $L_4 = 7890$
- Sibling of $P_2$ at Level 1: $P_1 = \text{BEEF}$

**Authentication Path:** $\text{Proof} = \{L_4, P_1\} = \{7890, \text{BEEF}\}$

**Step 2: Verification (verifier side).**

The verifier receives $D_3 = \text{"ADS"}$ and the proof $\{7890, \text{BEEF}\}$.

- **Step 2.1:** Compute the leaf hash:
$$L_3' = \text{Hash}(\text{"ADS"}) = 5E6F$$

- **Step 2.2:** Combine with sibling $L_4 = 7890$:
$$P_2' = \text{Hash}(5E6F \Vert 7890) = \text{Hash}(\text{"5E6F7890"}) = \text{CAFE}$$

- **Step 2.3:** Combine with sibling $P_1 = \text{BEEF}$:
$$R' = \text{Hash}(\text{BEEF} \Vert \text{CAFE}) = \text{Hash}(\text{"BEEFCAFE"}) = \text{DEAD}$$

- **Step 2.4:** Compare:
$$R' = \text{DEAD} = R_{\text{known}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{ACCEPT}$$

**Step 3: Proof size calculation.**

Number of hashes in the proof = $h = \lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$ hashes.
With SHA-256 output of 256 bits = 32 bytes:

$$\text{Proof size} = 2 \times 32 = 64 \text{ bytes}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the construction of a Merkle Tree for $n = 8$ data blocks. Derive the total number of hash operations required to build the tree and the time complexity of verifying any single block's membership. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Tree construction for $n = 8$.**

Given 8 data blocks $D_1, \dots, D_8$:
- **Level 0 (Leaves):** 8 leaf hashes: $L_i = \text{Hash}(D_i)$, for $i = 1, \dots, 8$.
- **Level 1:** 4 internal hashes: $P_j = \text{Hash}(L_{2j-1} \Vert L_{2j})$, for $j = 1, 2, 3, 4$.
- **Level 2:** 2 internal hashes: $Q_k = \text{Hash}(P_{2k-1} \Vert P_{2k})$, for $k = 1, 2$.
- **Level 3 (Root):** 1 hash: $R = \text{Hash}(Q_1 \Vert Q_2)$.

**Step 2: Derive total hash operations.**

$$T(8) = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7 = n - 1$$

Generalizing for $n = 2^h$:

$$T(n) = \frac{n}{2} + \frac{n}{4} + \dots + 1 = n - 1$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = n - 1 = O(n)}$$

**Step 3: Verification complexity.**

To verify $D_x$, recompute hashes from $L_x$ to $R$. Each level requires one hash.

$$\text{Levels} = h = \log_2 n = \log_2 8 = 3$$

$$\boxed{\text{Verification complexity} = O(\log n)}$$

---

**(b)** A distributed database uses Merkle trees for anti-entropy synchronization between two replicas. Each replica holds $n = 1{,}048{,}576$ ($= 2^{20}$) data blocks. Calculate: **(7 Marks)**

- **(i)** The height of the Merkle tree.
- **(ii)** The size of the Merkle proof (in bytes) using SHA-256.
- **(iii)** The total number of hash comparisons needed to detect a single divergent block.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Tree height:**

$$h = \log_2(2^{20}) = 20 \text{ levels}$$

**(ii) Merkle proof size:**

Number of hashes in proof = $h = 20$.

Each hash is 256 bits = 32 bytes.

$$\text{Proof size} = 20 \times 32 = 640 \text{ bytes}$$

**(iii) Hash comparisons for divergence detection:**

Two replicas compare hashes level by level. They start from the root:
- If root hashes match, all data is in sync (0 work).
- If root hashes differ, descend into differing subtrees.

**Worst case** (one block differs at the deepest leaf):
- Replicas must compare hashes at every level along the path.

$$\text{Comparisons} = h = 20$$

**Average case** (assuming random divergence):

$$\text{Comparisons} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{h} k \cdot 2^k \approx O(\log n)$$

**Conclusion:** The synchronization process requires only **$O(\log n) = 20$ hash comparisons** instead of $n = 1{,}048{,}576$ full block comparisons — a **~52,000× speedup**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Merkle Tree with Hash List:** A hash list is $O(n)$ to verify; a Merkle Tree is $O(\log n)$. Students often mix these up.
> 2. **Forgetting duplicate handling for odd $n$:** When $n$ is odd, the last leaf is typically duplicated. Skipping this yields incorrect root values in construction questions.
> 3. **Wrong proof size:** Writing "$n$ hashes" instead of "$\log_2 n$ hashes" loses full marks in part (a) of verification questions.
> 4. **Ignoring concatenation order:** The verifier must know whether the target hash is a left or right child. Many implementations include a "direction bit" with each sibling hash.
> 5. **Confusing Merkle Tree with Merkle DAG:** Merkle DAG (used in Git/IPFS) is a generalization allowing multi-parent nodes. A pure Merkle Tree is a strict tree.
> 6. **Not mentioning the cryptographic hash function:** Examiners expect explicit mention of SHA-256 or equivalent; a vague "hash function" loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 7. **Wrong complexity:** Writing $O(n)$ verification time is a critical error. The defining feature of Merkle Trees is $O(\log n)$ verification.
> 8. **Skipping the "Why":** Always justify *why* the structure is tamper-evident (any leaf change propagates to the root due to the cascade of hash recomputations).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Prep:**

- **Definition (1-liner):** A Merkle Tree is a binary hash tree where leaves are data hashes and internal nodes are hashes of concatenated child hashes.
- **Merkle Root:** The single top hash that uniquely summarizes all data blocks.
- **Leaf Hash:** $L_i = \text{Hash}(D_i)$ — one per data block.
- **Internal Hash:** $P = \text{Hash}(L_{\text{left}} \Vert L_{\text{right}})$ — recursive parent construction.
- **Merkle Proof:** Ordered set of sibling hashes from leaf to root.
- **Proof Size:** $O(\log n)$ hashes — **this is the whole point** of the data structure.
- **Verification Time:** $O(\log n)$ — logarithmic in number of blocks.
- **Build Time:** $O(n)$ with $n - 1$ total hash operations.
- **Storage:** $O(n)$ hash values, total $256(2n-1)$ bits for SHA-256.
- **Odd $n$ handling:** Duplicate the last unpaired hash at each level.
- **Cryptographic Hash Requirements:** Pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, collision resistance.
- **Standard Hash:** SHA-256 (Bitcoin, Git), Keccak-256 (Ethereum), BLAKE2 (high-performance).
- **Tamper-Evidence:** A single-bit change in any leaf changes the root due to cascading.
- **Applications to memorize:** Bitcoin block headers, Git object storage, IPFS, Certificate Transparency, Cassandra anti-entropy, DynamoDB.
- **Key Variants:** Binary Merkle Tree, Merkle Patricia Tree (Ethereum), Sparse Merkle Tree, Merkle Mountain Range (append-only).
- **Trade-off:** Tree is **immutable post-construction**; updates require full rebuild or rebalancing (this is why some systems use Merkle Mountain Ranges or versioned Merkle Trees).
- **Difference from Hash List:** Hash list verification is $O(n)$; Merkle Tree verification is $O(\log n)$ — this difference is frequently asked in KTU Part A questions.
- **Authentication Path ≠ Tree:** The path is $\log_2 n$ hashes, not $n$ or $2n - 1$.
- **Complexity Comparison Table to memorize:**

| Operation | Complexity |
|---|---|
| Tree construction | $O(n)$ |
| Hash computations for build | $n - 1$ |
| Merkle proof size | $O(\log n)$ |
| Verification time | $O(\log n)$ |
| Storage | $O(n)$ |
| Tamper detection | $O(1)$ via root mismatch |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications to Information Retrieval and WWW - Auto-Complete Using Tries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST495 - ADVANCED DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications to Information Retrieval and WWW - Auto-Complete Using Tries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auto-Complete Using Tries** is an information retrieval technique that leverages the **Trie (Prefix Tree / Digital Tree)** data structure to predict and suggest the most probable completions of a partially typed query in **O(L)** time, where **L** is the length of the input prefix.

In the context of the **World Wide Web (WWW)**, Auto-Complete (also called *type-ahead*, *incremental search*, or *word completion*) is the foundational user-experience feature deployed by search engines (Google, Bing, Yahoo), Integrated Development Environments (IDEs), command-line shells, and database query interfaces. KTU 2024 Scheme classifies this under **Information Retrieval (IR) Systems** because it reduces the cognitive load on the user and accelerates the document-query matching pipeline.

### Mathematical Formulation of Auto-Complete

Given a finite dictionary $\mathcal{D} = \{w_1, w_2, w_3, \ldots, w_n\}$ of $n$ valid indexed terms, and a user-typed prefix $p$ of length $\vert p \vert = L$, the Auto-Complete function $\mathcal{A}$ is formally defined as:

$$
\mathcal{A}(p, \mathcal{D}) = \{w \in \mathcal{D} \mid w[0 \ldots L-1] = p\}
$$

That is, $\mathcal{A}$ returns the **set of all words** $w$ in the dictionary whose first $L$ characters exactly match the prefix $p$. The result set is then ranked by frequency, recency, or a custom **TF-IDF / BM25 score** before being presented to the user.

### Glossary of Critical Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Trie Node** | A vertex in the Trie storing children pointers, an end-of-word flag, and optional metadata (frequency). |
| **Prefix Tree** | Synonym for Trie; an ordered tree where each node represents a single character. |
| **Root Node** | The empty-string ancestor; the universal entry point for all insertions and lookups. |
| **End-of-Word Marker** | A boolean flag (often `isTerminal`) that signals a complete dictionary word ends at that node. |
| **Alphabet $\Sigma$** | The finite character set (e.g., $\Sigma = \{a, \ldots, z\}$ or the full ASCII / Unicode table). |
| **Suggestion Set** | The ranked list of completions returned for a given prefix. |
| **Information Retrieval (IR)** | The activity of obtaining information resources relevant to an information need from a collection. |
| **Query Suggestion** | The WWW-specific term for Auto-Complete suggestions; powered by backend Trie or DAWG structures. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Library Card Catalogue (Reimagined for the 21st Century)**

Imagine you walk into a vast library containing **millions of books**, and a librarian asks: *"What topic are you researching?"* You mumble the first few letters — say, **"COM…"**

Instead of asking you to finish, the librarian immediately pulls out a special **phone-book-style index** that is organized **letter by letter**. They flip to the **C** drawer, then the **O** drawer, then the **M** drawer, and in **less than a second** they list out:

> *"Computing, Compiler, Communication, Commerce, Computational Biology, …"*

That magic index is a **Trie**.

- The **drawers** = the levels of the Trie (one level per character).
- The **phone-book entries inside a drawer** = child pointers.
- The **red star** marked on certain entries = the *end-of-word flag* (this drawer is a complete word).
- The **time it took** = exactly the number of letters you typed (linear in prefix length), **independent of how many millions of books are in the library**.

**Real-world deployment examples** (so the concept clicks immediately):
- **Google Search Bar** → the dropdown list as you type *"wea…"* shows *weather, weather.com, weather forecast*.
- **IDE IntelliSense** → typing `Syst.` in Java shows *System.out, System.in, System.arraycopy*.
- **Mobile T9 Keypad / Phone Contacts** → typing *274* suggests *Car, Bar, Cap*.
- **Command Line Shells (bash, zsh)** → pressing `Tab` autocompletes filenames.
- **Database SQL clients** → suggesting table/column names as you type JOIN clauses.

## 1.3 Why Tries Dominate Auto-Complete Over Alternatives

A student often asks: *"Why not just use a hash table or a sorted array?"* Here is the KTU-board-style comparison:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time-Complexity Verdict (The Deciding Factor)**
>
> | Operation | Sorted Array | Hash Table | BST | **Trie** |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Exact Lookup | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ avg | $O(\log n)$ avg | $O(L)$ |
> | **Prefix Lookup (Auto-Complete)** | $O(n)$ scan | **Not supported natively** | $O(n)$ worst | $\mathbf{O(L + k)}$ |
> | Ordered Traversal | $O(n)$ | Not supported | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
> | Memory per word | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(L \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ |
>
> where $k$ = number of suggestions returned, $L$ = prefix length, $n$ = dictionary size.
>
> The **Trie is the ONLY structure** that natively supports prefix search in time proportional to the *length of the prefix*, **not the size of the dictionary**. This is precisely why every major search engine uses a Trie (or its compressed variants — DAWG, Ternary Search Trie, Burst-Trie) as the backbone of its Auto-Complete subsystem.

## 1.4 Position of Auto-Complete in the WWW Information Retrieval Pipeline

The modern WWW information retrieval pipeline is layered as follows:

1. **Web Crawler** → fetches raw HTML documents.
2. **Indexer** → tokenizes documents and builds the inverted index.
3. **Query Processor** → receives user input.
4. **Auto-Complete Module (Trie-based)** → suggests completions BEFORE the user finishes typing.
5. **Ranking Engine (PageRank + BM25 + Learning-to-Rank)** → ranks final results.
6. **Snippet Generator** → produces the SERP (Search Engine Results Page).

Our focus in Module 2 is **Stage 4** — the Trie-powered Auto-Complete subsystem that sits at the front door of every search interaction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Trie Data Structure: Operational Anatomy

A Trie of order $\vert \Sigma \vert$ is a rooted tree in which:

- The **root** is associated with the empty string $\epsilon$.
- Every edge is labelled with exactly one character $c \in \Sigma$.
- Each node $v$ at depth $d$ represents the string formed by concatenating the edge labels on the unique path from the root to $v$.
- A node may carry a boolean flag $\text{isEndOfWord}(v) \in \{\text{true}, \text{false}\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** A node's *existence* in the Trie does NOT mean a word ends there. Words can PASS THROUGH a node. Only the $\text{isEndOfWord}$ flag marks terminal words. Many students lose marks in KTU exams for confusing "node exists" with "word exists."

### Formal Trie Node Definition

$$
\text{Node} = \langle \text{children} : \Sigma \rightarrow \text{Node}^*, \quad \text{isEndOfWord} : \mathbb{B}, \quad \text{metadata} : \mathcal{M} \rangle
$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ is an optional metadata payload (frequency count, document IDs, etc.).

## 2.2 Operational Logic: Step-by-Step Execution of Auto-Complete

### Algorithm: INSERT(word, root)

1. Set current node $v \leftarrow \text{root}$.
2. **For** each character $c$ in word (left to right):
   - **If** $c \notin v.\text{children}$: create a new node $u$, set $v.\text{children}[c] \leftarrow u$.
   - Set $v \leftarrow v.\text{children}[c]$.
3. Set $v.\text{isEndOfWord} \leftarrow \text{true}$.
4. Increment $v.\text{frequency}$ (if frequency-based ranking is used).

### Algorithm: AUTOCOMPLETE(prefix, root) → List of Suggestions

1. Set current node $v \leftarrow \text{root}$.
2. **For** each character $c$ in prefix:
   - **If** $c \notin v.\text{children}$: **return** $\emptyset$ (no suggestions possible).
   - Set $v \leftarrow v.\text{children}[c]$.
3. **Perform DFS / pre-order traversal** of the subtree rooted at $v$.
4. **Whenever** a node $u$ with $\text{isEndOfWord}(u) = \text{true}$ is visited, append the path string to the result list.
5. **Return** the collected list, optionally sorted by $\text{frequency}$ descending.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for the KTU ESE. Every line is a potential 3-mark or 7-mark sub-question.**

| # | Property | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time — Insertion of one word of length $L$** | $O(L \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ | Worst case visits $L$ nodes; array lookup is $O(1)$ |
| 2 | **Time — Exact Search of length $L$** | $O(L)$ | Independent of dictionary size |
| 3 | **Time — Auto-Complete (prefix of length $L$, $k$ results)** | $O(L + k)$ | Walk down $L$ levels, then traverse $k$ terminal nodes |
| 4 | **Space — Single word of length $L$** | $O(L \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ | Each level allocates a $\vert\Sigma\vert$-sized child array |
| 5 | **Space — Full Trie of $n$ words, total chars $N$** | $O(N \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ | Worst case (no shared prefixes) |
| 6 | **Space — Practical English dictionary** | $\approx 25 \cdot N$ nodes | Empirically validated |
| 7 | **Time — Deletion of one word** | $O(L)$ | Recursive prune if no other word uses the suffix |
| 8 | **Alphabet size for lowercase English** | $\vert\Sigma\vert = 26$ | Standard exam assumption |
| 9 | **Alphabet size for full ASCII** | $\vert\Sigma\vert = 128$ | Used when case + symbols are indexed |
| 10 | **Alphabet size for Unicode (UTF-8)** | $\vert\Sigma\vert \leq 1{,}114{,}112$ | Theoretical; practical engines use B-trees here |
| 11 | **Height of Trie** | $h = \max(L_i)$ | Equal to the longest word in the dictionary |
| 12 | **Compressed Trie (Patricia) space** | $O(N)$ | Stores single edges instead of character-by-character levels |
| 13 | **Ternary Search Trie lookup** | $O(L \cdot \log \vert\Sigma\vert)$ | Uses 3-child BST structure per node |

### Critical Boundary Conditions

- **Empty Prefix ($p = \epsilon$):** Auto-Complete returns ALL words in the dictionary (DFS from root).
- **Prefix Longer Than Any Word:** Returns $\emptyset$ in $O(L)$.
- **Single-Character Dictionary:** Trie degenerates to a list-like structure of depth 1.
- **All Words Share Same Prefix:** Only the root-to-shared-node path is materialized; massive memory savings vs storing each word individually.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real-World WWW Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight (Frequently Asked in KTU Viva)**
>
> 1. **Latency Budget:** Google's Auto-Complete must return within **100–200 ms**. A linear scan of $n = 10^9$ queries is impossible; a Trie with $O(L + k) \leq O(50 + 10) = O(60)$ operations is the only feasible choice.
> 2. **Memory Pressure:** Google stores $\approx 200$ billion Auto-Complete suggestions. A naive array-per-node Trie ($\vert\Sigma\vert = 26$ children pointers) would consume **petabytes**. Hence the industry uses **compressed variants**: DAWGs, Burst-Tries, and Finite-State Transducers (FSTs) — all derived from the basic Trie concept covered in your KTU syllabus.
> 3. **Personalization Layer:** Modern Auto-Complete also overlays user history (location, prior queries) on top of the static Trie. The Trie provides the candidate set; a re-ranker scores the candidates.
> 4. **Spelling Tolerance:** Real systems combine the Trie with **Levenshtein Automata** so that *"wether"* still suggests *"weather"*. This is an advanced KTU Module 2 extension.

## 2.5 Compressed Trie Variants (Syllabus-Aware)

KTU 2024 Module 2 expects you to know the *existence and motivation* of these variants:

### A. Compressed Trie (Patricia Tree / Radix Tree)
- Merges chains of single-child nodes into a single edge labelled with a substring.
- Space: $O(N)$ instead of $O(N \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$.

### B. Ternary Search Trie (TST)
- Each node has 3 children: *less-than*, *equal*, *greater-than* (BST-style on character).
- Memory: $O(N)$ pointers; lookup: $O(L \cdot \log \vert\Sigma\vert)$.

### C. Directed Acyclic Word Graph (DAWG)
- A minimal deterministic finite automaton recognizing the dictionary.
- Edges share suffixes, achieving minimum-state storage.
- Used in classic search engines (e.g., early Lucene, Spellbook).

### D. Burst-Trie
- A Trie in which high-degree leaf nodes are replaced with a container (e.g., a BST or hash table) for the words.
- Best of both worlds for streaming text.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1: Building a Trie from a Dictionary

**Problem (KTU-style):** Insert the following dictionary into an initially empty Trie and show the state after every insertion:

$$
\mathcal{D} = \{\text{"car"}, \text{"card"}, \text{"care"}, \text{"carbon"}, \text{"dog"}, \text{"do"}\}
$$

Assume $\Sigma = \{a, b, c, d, e, g, i, n, o, r\}$ (lowercase English).

### Step 1 — Insert "car"

Path: root → c → a → r
- Create node 'c' under root.
- Create node 'a' under 'c'.
- Create node 'r' under 'a'.
- Mark node 'r' as $\text{isEndOfWord} = \text{true}$.

### Step 2 — Insert "card"

Path: root → c → a → r → d
- Nodes c, a, r already exist (no creation).
- Create new node 'd' under 'r'.
- Mark node 'd' as $\text{isEndOfWord} = \text{true}$.
- Note: node 'r' is **BOTH** an intermediate node AND an end-of-word marker for "car".

### Step 3 — Insert "care"

Path: root → c → a → r → e
- Nodes c, a, r exist.
- Create new node 'e' under 'r'.
- Mark node 'e' as $\text{isEndOfWord} = \text{true}$.

### Step 4 — Insert "carbon"

Path: root → c → a → r → b → o → n
- Nodes c, a, r exist.
- Create new node 'b' under 'r'.
- Create new node 'o' under 'b'.
- Create new node 'n' under 'o'.
- Mark node 'n' as $\text{isEndOfWord} = \text{true}$.

### Step 5 — Insert "dog"

Path: root → d → o → g
- Create node 'd' under root.
- Create node 'o' under 'd'.
- Create node 'g' under 'o'.
- Mark node 'g' as $\text{isEndOfWord} = \text{true}$.

### Step 6 — Insert "do"

Path: root → d → o
- Nodes d, o exist.
- Mark node 'o' (under d) as $\text{isEndOfWord} = \text{true}$.

### Final Trie (Textual Representation)

```
                (root)
               /      \
             c*        d*
            / \         \
           a*  .        o*
          / \          / \
         r*  .        g*  (terminal for "do")
        /|\           
       d* e*          
       |  |            
       .  b*           
           \           
            o*         
             \         
              n*       
```

(The `*` denotes $\text{isEndOfWord} = \text{true}$.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Count (Common KTU Sub-Question):**
>
> - Total nodes created = **12**
> - Total characters in dictionary = $3 + 4 + 4 + 6 + 3 + 2 = 22$
> - Naive storage (each word in its own array) = $22$ cells
> - Trie storage = $12$ nodes $\times \vert\Sigma\vert$ pointers each
> - Memory savings arise from **shared prefixes** like "car-", "do-".

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2: Auto-Complete Query Execution

**Problem:** Using the Trie from Example 1, perform Auto-Complete for the prefix $p =$ **"ca"** and list all completions in lexicographic order.

### Step 1 — Traverse the prefix

Start at root. Walk 'c' → exists, descend. Walk 'a' → exists, descend. We are now at the node representing "ca".

### Step 2 — Initiate DFS from the "ca" node

We perform a **pre-order DFS** of the subtree rooted at the "ca" node. The traversal visits:

| Visit Order | Node | Path String | $\text{isEndOfWord}$? | Add to Result? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | r | "car" | $\text{true}$ | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | d | "card" | $\text{true}$ | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | e | "care" | $\text{true}$ | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | b | "carb" | $\text{false}$ | ❌ No (skip subtree leaf traversal continues) |
| 5 | o | "carbo" | $\text{false}$ | ❌ No |
| 6 | n | "carbon" | $\text{true}$ | ✅ Yes |

### Step 3 — Compile the final result set

$$
\mathcal{A}(\text{"ca"}, \mathcal{D}) = \{\text{"car"}, \text{"card"}, \text{"care"}, \text{"carbon"}\}
$$

If a **frequency-based ranking** is applied (assume frequencies: car=10, card=5, care=3, carbon=7), the ranked Auto-Complete dropdown would be:

$$
\text{car (10)} \succ \text{carbon (7)} \succ \text{card (5)} \succ \text{care (3)}
$$

### Step 4 — Time Complexity Justification (For Full Marks)

The traversal visited $L = 2$ levels to reach the prefix, then descended into the subtree. The number of nodes visited during DFS is bounded by the number of characters in all matching words (plus 1 per non-terminal internal node). In the worst case this is $O(k \cdot L_{\max})$ where $k$ is the number of results. Hence the total time is:

$$
T_{\text{AutoComplete}}(p, \mathcal{D}) = O(L) + O(\text{subtree traversal}) = O(L + k)
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Time-Complexity Derivations

### Derivation 1: Insertion Time

Let $w$ be a word of length $L = \vert w \vert$. Insertion walks one edge per character. Each edge lookup takes $O(1)$ (array index) or $O(\log \vert\Sigma\vert)$ (BST-style child map) or $O(\vert\Sigma\vert)$ (linear scan of children). Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{insert}}(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} T_{\text{child\_lookup}}(c_i) = L \cdot O(1) = O(L)
$$

When using an array-of-pointers per node, the constant factor is $\vert\Sigma\vert$, but the asymptotic complexity remains $O(L)$.

### Derivation 2: Exact Search Time

Identical to insertion, except we **fail fast** when a child is missing. In the worst case (word exists), all $L$ characters are inspected:

$$
T_{\text{search}}(w) = O(L)
$$

Crucially, this is **independent of $n$**, the dictionary size. This is the killer feature of the Trie.

### Derivation 3: Auto-Complete (Prefix Search) Time

Let $p$ be the prefix, $L = \vert p \vert$, and let $k$ be the number of words in the dictionary that begin with $p$.

1. Walk down $L$ levels: $O(L)$.
2. DFS the subtree, visiting every node along every matching word's path: bounded by $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert w_i \vert \leq k \cdot L_{\max}$.

$$
T_{\text{autocomplete}}(p) = O(L) + O(k \cdot L_{\max}) = O(L + k)
$$

when $L_{\max}$ is treated as a dictionary constant.

### Derivation 4: Space Complexity

Each node stores:
- $\vert\Sigma\vert$ child pointers
- 1 boolean flag
- Optional metadata (frequency, etc.)

Total pointers allocated = $\sum_{v \in \text{Trie}} \vert\Sigma\vert = \vert\text{Trie}\vert \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert$.

In the worst case, the Trie has one node per character in the dictionary, so $\vert\text{Trie}\vert = N = \sum_{w \in \mathcal{D}} \vert w \vert$. Thus:

$$
S_{\text{Trie}} = O(N \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)
$$

### Derivation 5: Why Hash Tables Lose for Auto-Complete

A hash table can test membership in $O(1)$ but **cannot enumerate all keys sharing a prefix** without scanning ALL keys. Formally, given a hash table of $n$ keys and a prefix $p$:

$$
T_{\text{hash\_prefix}} = O(n) \quad \text{(must scan all entries to test } w[0..L-1] = p)
$$

This is because the hash function destroys the ordering of characters; it maps "car" and "carbon" to totally unrelated buckets. Hence the hash table is **structurally incapable** of answering prefix queries faster than linear scan.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Edge Cases & Failure Modes

| Edge Case | Trie Behavior | Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Empty string as prefix | Return all words | Filter by relevance score |
| Prefix is the entire word | Return the word + any extensions (e.g., "car" → "car", "card", "care") | Standard expected behavior |
| Unicode / multi-byte input | Each character may be multi-byte; node must be UTF-8 aware | Use ICU library or byte-level Trie |
| Case sensitivity | "Car" ≠ "car" in case-sensitive Trie | Store a normalized lowercase form |
| Very deep prefix | Degenerate to linked list, but still $O(L)$ | Acceptable; depth $\leq 50$ for real queries |
| Massively skewed alphabet | $\vert\Sigma\vert = 26$ wastes 25 pointers per letter 'q' | Switch to TST or hash-map children |

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Auto-Complete Subsystem in a Search Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Keystroke Event] --> B[Input Sanitization Layer]
    B --> C{Prefix Validator}
    C -->|Invalid Empty| D[No Suggestion State]
    C -->|Valid Prefix p| E[Trie Walker Module]
    E --> F{Trie Node Exists?}
    F -->|No| G[Return Empty Suggestion Set]
    F -->|Yes| H[Subtree DFS Traversal]
    H --> I[Suggestion Collector]
    I --> J[Frequency Ranker]
    J --> K[Personalization Re-ranker]
    K --> L[Spelling Tolerance Filter]
    L --> M[Result Cache Lookup]
    M --> N[Final Suggestion Dropdown UI]
    M --> O[Cache Update Write Back]
    H --> P[Subtree Path String Builder]
    P --> I
```

**Figure 4.1 — End-to-End Auto-Complete Pipeline.** The Trie Walker Module is the data-structure-centric core; everything else is engineering scaffolding.

## 4.3 State Machine for Trie Walk + DFS Auto-Complete

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Typing: User types character
    Typing --> PrefixWalk: Pass prefix to Trie
    PrefixWalk --> CharMatch: Edge exists
    PrefixWalk --> NoMatch: Edge missing
    CharMatch --> PrefixWalk: More characters remain
    CharMatch --> DFSStart: All prefix chars consumed
    NoMatch --> EmptyResult: Return no suggestions
    DFSStart --> CollectNode: Visit subtree node
    CollectNode --> IsTerminal: Check isEndOfWord flag
    IsTerminal --> AppendToResults: Flag is true
    IsTerminal --> ContinueDFS: Flag is false
    AppendToResults --> ContinueDFS
    ContinueDFS --> DFSStart: More unvisited nodes
    ContinueDFS --> RankResults: Traversal complete
    RankResults --> DisplayDropdown: Send to UI
    DisplayDropdown --> Idle: Await next keystroke
    EmptyResult --> DisplayEmpty: Show no suggestions
    DisplayEmpty --> Idle
```

**Figure 4.3 — Lifecycle State Machine.** Captures the entire Auto-Complete flow from keystroke to dropdown display, including failure handling.

---

## 4.4 Memory Layout Comparison: Trie vs Alternatives

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:trieBlock:Trie Memory
        T1["Per-node: 26 child pointers"]
        T2["1 boolean flag"]
        T3["Optional frequency counter"]
    end
    block:hashBlock:Hash Table Memory
        H1["Array of n buckets"]
        H2["Each entry: key + value"]
        H3["No structural prefix info"]
    end
    block:bstBlock:BST Memory
        B1["Per-node: 2 child pointers"]
        B2["1 key + value"]
        B3["No native prefix support"]
    end
```

**Figure 4.4 — Memory Footprint Trade-off.** The Trie pays a higher constant-factor memory cost ($\vert\Sigma\vert$ pointers per node) in exchange for **native prefix query support**, which neither hash tables nor BSTs provide.
