# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**Q: Define a Trie data structure. Why is it preferred over hash tables for implementing Auto-Complete features in web search engines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Trie, also known as a prefix tree, is a tree-based data structure used for storing a dynamic set of strings where each node represents a single character, and the path from the root to any node represents a string prefix. Each node maintains child pointers for every possible character in the alphabet $\Sigma$ and an end-of-word marker.

It is preferred over hash tables for Auto-Complete because **(1)** a Trie supports prefix-based searching in $O(L)$ time independent of dictionary size $n$, while hash tables would require $O(n)$ time to scan all entries; **(2)** Tries naturally enumerate all words sharing a common prefix, which is the exact requirement of an Auto-Complete dropdown, whereas hash tables destroy character-level ordering during hashing; **(3)** Trie traversals can be cached, indexed, and combined with frequency metadata to produce ranked suggestions efficiently.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**Q: List any three real-world applications of the Trie data structure in Information Retrieval and the World Wide Web. Mention the time complexity of the Auto-Complete operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three real-world applications are:
1. **Search engine query Auto-Complete** (Google, Bing) — suggests completions as the user types.
2. **IP routing (Longest Prefix Matching)** — routers use a compressed Trie (binary trie) to find the longest matching routing prefix in $O(W)$ where $W$ is the address width (32 or 128 bits).
3. **Spell checkers and dictionary-based T9 word prediction** — mobile phones use Tries to suggest words from numeric keypresses.
4. *(Bonus 4th application)* **Bioinformatics DNA/RNA pattern matching** — Tries index large genomes for $k$-mer lookup.

The time complexity of the Auto-Complete operation is $O(L + k)$, where $L$ is the length of the input prefix and $k$ is the number of valid completions returned.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** Construct a Trie by inserting the following words into an initially empty Trie in the given order. Show the final Trie structure clearly and indicate which nodes are end-of-word markers. Dictionary: `{apple, app, apex, ape, apt, apply, bear, bid, big}`

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** Using the Trie built in part (a), perform the Auto-Complete operation for the prefix "ap" and list all returned suggestions. Show the traversal path explicitly and compute the time complexity of the operation.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Insert "apple":** Creates nodes a → p → p → l → e. Node 'e' marked terminal.

**Step 2 — Insert "app":** Path a → p → p exists. Mark the second 'p' as terminal.

**Step 3 — Insert "apex":** From terminal 'p' (depth 3), create child 'e', then child 'x'. Mark 'x' terminal.

**Step 4 — Insert "ape":** From node 'e' (depth 4), mark it as terminal. (It already has child 'x'.)

**Step 5 — Insert "apt":** From terminal 'p' (depth 3), create child 't'. Mark 't' terminal.

**Step 6 — Insert "apply":** From node 'p' (depth 3, first), traverse to 'l' (exists), then to 'y' (create). Mark 'y' terminal.

**Step 7 — Insert "bear":** Create 'b' under root, then 'e', 'a', 'r'. Mark 'r' terminal.

**Step 8 — Insert "bid":** Create 'b' (exists), then 'i', then 'd'. Mark 'd' terminal.

**Step 9 — Insert "big":** From 'i' (depth 2), create 'g'. Mark 'g' terminal.

**Final Trie (textual):**

```
(root)
├── a
│   └── p  (terminal: "app")
│       ├── p
│       │   ├── l
│       │   │   ├── e  (terminal: "apple")
│       │   │   └── y  (terminal: "apply")
│       ├── e
│       │   ├── (terminal: "ape")
│       │   └── x  (terminal: "apex")
│       └── t  (terminal: "apt")
└── b
    ├── e
    │   └── a
    │       └── r  (terminal: "bear")
    └── i
        ├── d  (terminal: "bid")
        └── g  (terminal: "big")
```

**Node Count:** Total nodes = 1 (root) + 15 = **16 nodes**.

**Terminal nodes:** "app", "apple", "apply", "ape", "apex", "apt", "bear", "bid", "big" = **9 terminal nodes**.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Prefix walk for "ap":** root → 'a' (exists) → 'p' (exists, depth 2). Time so far: $O(2)$.

**Step 2 — DFS from the 'p' node at depth 2:**

| Visit # | Path | isEndOfWord? | Add to List? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | app | true | ✅ |
| 2 | apple | true | ✅ |
| 3 | apply | true | ✅ |
| 4 | ape | true | ✅ |
| 5 | apex | true | ✅ |
| 6 | apt | true | ✅ |

**Step 3 — Final Suggestion Set:**

$$
\mathcal{A}(\text{"ap"}, \mathcal{D}) = \{\text{"app"}, \text{"apple"}, \text{"apply"}, \text{"ape"}, \text{"apex"}, \text{"apt"}\}
$$

If frequencies are tied, the natural DFS pre-order yields this exact order.

**Step 4 — Time Complexity:**

The walk down took $L = 2$ steps. The DFS visited 6 terminal nodes plus 1 internal branching node. The total cost is:

$$
T_{\text{autocomplete}}(\text{"ap"}) = O(L + k) = O(2 + 6) = O(8)
$$

In Big-O notation, this simplifies to $O(L + k)$ where $L$ = prefix length and $k$ = number of results.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** Compare the Trie data structure with Hash Tables and Binary Search Trees (BST) for implementing an Auto-Complete system. Use a comparison table covering insertion, exact lookup, prefix lookup, and memory usage.

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** A search engine has a dictionary of 5 million words. The average word length is 8 characters and the alphabet size is 26 lowercase English letters. Estimate the worst-case space required by a Trie assuming each child pointer occupies 4 bytes and the end-of-word flag occupies 1 byte. Also, suggest ONE optimization to reduce this memory footprint.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Hash Table | BST | **Trie** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Insertion | $O(1)$ avg, $O(n)$ worst | $O(\log n)$ avg | $O(L)$ |
| Exact Lookup | $O(1)$ avg, $O(n)$ worst | $O(\log n)$ avg | $O(L)$ |
| **Prefix Lookup (Auto-Complete)** | $O(n)$ — must scan all keys | $O(n)$ — must visit all nodes | $\mathbf{O(L + k)}$ |
| Ordered Traversal | Not supported | $O(n)$ in-order | $O(n)$ DFS |
| Memory per entry | $O(L)$ for key | $O(L)$ + 2 pointers | $O(L \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ |
| Supports ranking of prefix matches | No | No | Yes (via frequency metadata) |
| Cache-friendly | Bucket arrays are | Pointer-chasing, poor | Pointer-chasing, poor |

**Verdict (3 marks):** For Auto-Complete, the **Trie is the only data structure** that natively supports prefix enumeration in time proportional to the prefix length, not the dictionary size. Hash tables destroy character ordering during hashing, making prefix queries a full $O(n)$ scan. BSTs preserve lexicographic order but still require a full in-order traversal filtered by prefix check at every node. Therefore, the Trie is the optimal choice for Auto-Complete, despite its higher constant-factor memory overhead.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Number of words: $n = 5 \times 10^6$
- Average word length: $L = 8$
- Alphabet size: $\vert\Sigma\vert = 26$
- Pointer size: 4 bytes
- End-of-word flag size: 1 byte

**Step 1 — Total number of characters in the dictionary:**

$$
N = n \times L = 5 \times 10^6 \times 8 = 4 \times 10^7 \text{ characters}
$$

**Step 2 — Worst-case number of Trie nodes (no shared prefixes):**

$$
\text{Nodes}_{\max} = N = 4 \times 10^7
$$

**Step 3 — Memory per node in worst case:**

$$
M_{\text{node}} = (\vert\Sigma\vert \times 4 \text{ bytes}) + 1 \text{ byte} = (26 \times 4) + 1 = 104 + 1 = 105 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Total worst-case memory:**

$$
M_{\text{Trie}} = \text{Nodes}_{\max} \times M_{\text{node}} = 4 \times 10^7 \times 105 = 4.2 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}
$$

Converting to gigabytes:

$$
M_{\text{Trie}} = \frac{4.2 \times 10^9}{1024^3} \approx 3.91 \text{ GB}
$$

(If using decimal GB: $4.2 \times 10^9 / 10^9 = 4.2$ GB.)

**Step 5 — Optimization to reduce memory (3 marks):**

**Recommended Optimization: Compressed Trie (Patricia Tree) or Ternary Search Trie (TST).**

A **Compressed Trie / Patricia Tree** merges chains of single-child nodes into a single edge labelled with a substring. This reduces the node count from $O(N \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ to $O(N)$, and the memory footprint drops from $\approx 4$ GB to roughly:

$$
M_{\text{Patricia}} \approx N \times 64 \text{ bytes} = 4 \times 10^7 \times 64 = 2.56 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes} \approx 2.4 \text{ GB}
$$

A more aggressive option is the **Ternary Search Trie (TST)**, which uses only 3 child pointers per node plus 1 character, dropping memory to roughly:

$$
M_{\text{TST}} \approx N \times (3 \times 4 + 1) = N \times 13 \approx 0.52 \text{ GB}
$$

This is why real search engines deploy TSTs, DAWGs, and FSTs in production.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Auto-Complete Trie Questions (Read Before Writing Your Exam!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the end-of-word flag:** A node may exist for a character that is an intermediate letter, NOT a complete word. Many students mark all nodes as words, losing 1–2 marks per sub-part.
> 2. **Confusing "node exists" with "word exists":** A common error is to list every node in the subtree as a "suggestion." Only nodes with $\text{isEndOfWord} = \text{true}$ are valid completions.
> 3. **Writing $O(1)$ for Auto-Complete:** Students sometimes state that hash tables give $O(1)$ lookup and conclude that Auto-Complete is $O(1)$. **WRONG.** Auto-Complete is a *prefix* query, not a *membership* query. The correct complexity is $O(L + k)$ for a Trie and $O(n)$ for a hash table.
> 4. **Not showing traversal paths explicitly:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for clearly walking the prefix and showing the DFS visits. A bare final answer without the work loses those marks.
> 5. **Wrong alphabet size:** For lowercase English, $\vert\Sigma\vert = 26$ (not 52, not 256). Using the wrong value corrupts every memory calculation downstream.
> 6. **Mixing up Big-O with byte counts:** $O(N \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ is asymptotic; the examiner wants the **actual byte count** in questions that give concrete sizes.
> 7. **Forgetting to compute total time for Auto-Complete:** The walk-down is $O(L)$ AND the DFS is $O(k \cdot L_{\max})$. Students often mention only one of the two phases.
> 8. **Spelling "Trie" incorrectly:** It is pronounced "try" (from retrieval) and is sometimes misspelled as "Try", "Tri", or "Tree" in answers. Always write **Trie**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read This the Night Before the KTU ESE)**
>
> ✅ **Core Definition:** A Trie is a tree-based data structure that stores strings character-by-character, enabling $O(L)$ prefix search.
>
> ✅ **Auto-Complete Time:** $O(L + k)$ where $L$ = prefix length, $k$ = number of completions.
>
> ✅ **Insertion & Search Time:** $O(L)$ independent of dictionary size $n$.
>
> ✅ **Space (Worst Case):** $O(N \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ where $N$ = total characters in dictionary.
>
> ✅ **End-of-Word Flag:** Mandatory marker; distinguishes terminal words from intermediate prefixes.
>
> ✅ **Why Not Hash Table?** Hash tables cannot natively answer prefix queries; they require $O(n)$ scan.
>
> ✅ **Why Not BST?** BSTs preserve order but still need full traversal with prefix filtering — $O(n)$ worst case.
>
> ✅ **Compressed Variants:** Patricia Tree (Radix Tree), Ternary Search Trie, DAWG, Burst-Trie — all reduce memory.
>
> ✅ **Alphabet Size:** $|\Sigma| = 26$ for lowercase English (standard KTU assumption).
>
> ✅ **WWW Applications:** Search engine Auto-Complete, IP routing (longest prefix match), spell checkers, T9 prediction, bioinformatics.
>
> ✅ **Traversal Algorithm:** DFS (pre-order) from the prefix node; collect every node with $\text{isEndOfWord} = \text{true}$.
>
> ✅ **Memory Calculation Formula:** $\text{Nodes} \times (\vert\Sigma\vert \cdot 4 \text{ bytes} + 1 \text{ byte flag})$.
>
> ✅ **Real-World Constraint:** Google processes billions of suggestions; hence the use of FSTs and Burst-Tries in production (NOT naive array-per-node Tries).
>
> ✅ **Examiner's Hot Topic:** Questions on Auto-Complete + Trie are repeated almost every KTU cycle — expect at least one 7-mark or 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specialized Data Structures - Spatial Data Structures - Quadtree, K-D Trees (K-Dimensional Tree)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST495 - ADVANCED DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Specialized Data Structures - Spatial Data Structures - Quadtree, K-D Trees (K-Dimensional Tree) # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Spatial Data Structures? A **Spatial Data Structure** is a specialized data structure designed specifically to store, manage, query, and manipulate geometric or spatial objects — typically points, lines, rectangles, polygons, or higher-dimensional regions. Unlike linear data structures (arrays, lists) and traditional tree structures (BST, AVL), spatial data structures exploit the *geometric locality* of data to perform operations such as **range search, nearest-neighbor search, and region queries** efficiently. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *Spatial data structures are tree-based or hierarchical structures that recursively partition a multi-dimensional space to enable efficient storage, retrieval, and analysis of geometric data.* ### 1.2 Classification of Spatial Data Structures Spatial data structures are broadly classified as: | Category | Examples | Dimensionality | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Point-based Partitioning** | K-D Tree, BSP Tree | k-dim | Nearest neighbor, range search | | **Space-based Partitioning** | Quadtree, Octree, R-Tree | 2D / 3D | Image compression, GIS, collision detection | | **Hybrid** | R+-Tree, R*-Tree, KD-B-Tree | Multi | Database indexing | --- ## 1.3 The Quadtree ### Formal Definition A **Quadtree** is a hierarchical tree-based spatial data structure in which each **internal node has exactly four children**, denoted conventionally as **NW (North-West), NE (North-East), SW (South-West), and SE (South-East)**. The 2D space is recursively subdivided into four equal (or sometimes weighted) quadrants until a termination condition is met (e.g., uniform region, capacity threshold, or empty region). > [!NOTE] > The term **"Quadtree"** was originally coined by **Raphael Finkel and Jon Bentley (1974)** in the context of representing 2D region data. The structure has since become foundational in computer graphics, GIS, and image processing. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Dividing Map" > [!TIP] > **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you have a large city map with millions of delivery locations scattered across it. Finding "all deliveries within 2 km of a customer" by scanning the whole list is painfully slow. A Quadtree solves this by repeatedly **chopping the map into 4 smaller quadrants** (like cutting a pizza into quarters, then cutting one quarter again, and so on). Areas with no deliveries are *pruned away*. When you later need to answer a query, you only explore the *relevant* quadrants — drastically reducing work. ### Types of Quadtrees (KTU-Relevant Variants) | Variant | Stored Object | Termination Condition | Primary Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Point Quadtree** | 2D Points | Node capacity reached | Spatial point indexing | | **Region Quadtree** | 2D Image regions | Uniform pixel block | Image compression | | **PR-Quadtree** (Point-Region) | Points with capacity *b* | Bucket overflow | Database point storage | | **MX-Quadtree** | Points with integer coords | Leaf homogeneity | VLSI CAD, raster graphics | --- ## 1.4 The K-Dimensional Tree (K-D Tree) ### Formal Definition A **K-Dimensional Tree (K-D Tree)** is a **binary space-partitioning** data structure that generalizes the Binary Search Tree (BST) to **k-dimensional** data. Each node of a K-D tree represents a single *k-dimensional point*. At any given level $\ell$ of the tree, the splitting dimension is computed as $d = \ell \bmod k$, meaning the tree **cycles through the k dimensions** as it descends level by level. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inventor:** **Jon Louis Bentley (1975)** introduced the K-D tree in his seminal paper *"Multidimensional Binary Search Trees Used for Associative Searching"*, Communications of the ACM, 18(9), pp. 509–517. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "20-Questions Game in Multiple Dimensions" > [!TIP] > **Intuition (Plain English):** Picture the game "20 Questions" where you narrow down a hidden object by asking yes/no questions. In a normal BST, you ask questions only about *one* dimension (e.g., "is x < 50?"). In a K-D tree, you **rotate** the question with each step: "is x < 50? … is y < 30? … is z < 70? … back to x again!". This *cycling of dimensions* is the key trick that makes K-D trees excel at multi-dimensional searches like *"find the nearest coffee shop"*. ### Key Differences: Quadtree vs. K-D Tree | Property | Quadtree | K-D Tree | |---|---|---| | **Branching factor** | 4 (in 2D), 8 (in 3D → Octree) | Always 2 (binary) | | **Partitioning** | Space-based (equal region split) | Data-based (median-based) | | **Dimensions** | Typically 2D / 3D | Arbitrary k | | **Balance** | Not necessarily balanced | Balanced (if median splitting used) | | **Best for** | Region/image data, uniform splits | Point sets, NN-search, k-NN | | **Worst-case height** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | | **Average height** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Metric:** Both structures are evaluated against operations *insert, search, delete, range query,* and *nearest-neighbor query*, with the average-case complexity being **O(log n)** and worst-case being **O(n)**. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Quadtree — Operational Decomposition ### 2.1.1 Node Structure A Quadtree node (in C-style pseudocode) is structured as: ```c struct QuadtreeNode { Point point; // payload data (for point quadtree) Rectangle bounds; // spatial region this node owns int capacity; // max points before split (PR-quadtree) int count; // current point count struct QuadtreeNode *NW; // North-West child struct QuadtreeNode *NE; // North-East child struct QuadtreeNode *SW; // South-West child struct QuadtreeNode *SE; // South-East child bool isLeaf; // flag for leaf status }; ``` ### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Step 1 — *Root Creation*** - The root node is created with bounds equal to the **entire 2D space** (e.g., $[0, 2^L) \times [0, 2^L)$ for an $L$-level PR-Quadtree). **Step 2 — *Insertion Logic*** - If the current node is a **leaf with free capacity** → store the point. - If the leaf is **full** (count == capacity) → **subdivide** the region into 4 equal quadrants and re-distribute existing points into the appropriate children, then insert the new point. **Step 3 — *Quadrant Determination*** Given a node's center $(c_x, c_y)$ and an incoming point $(p_x, p_y)$: - If $p_x \leq c_x$ **and** $p_y \geq c_y$ → **NW** quadrant - If $p_x > c_x$ **and** $p_y \geq c_y$ → **NE** quadrant - If $p_x \leq c_x$ **and** $p_y < c_y$ → **SW** quadrant - If $p_x > c_x$ **and** $p_y < c_y$ → **SE** quadrant > [!IMPORTANT] > The "Y-up" convention is used here (typical for computer graphics). For mathematical (Y-down) coordinate systems, swap the inequality signs on $p_y$. **Step 4 — *Search (Range Query)*** - For a query rectangle $R$, traverse only those quadrants whose **bounding box intersects $R$**. Recurse into intersecting children. Skip entire subtrees that lie fully outside $R$. **Step 5 — *Deletion*** - Find and remove the point, then optionally **merge** sibling subtrees that now have $\leq$ capacity points (to control tree height). ### 2.1.3 Why Quadtree Works — The "Why" The efficiency of the Quadtree stems from the principle of **spatial locality of reference**: nearby points in 2D space are likely to be co-located in the same sub-tree, so queries about a region only need to descend into relevant subtrees. The 4-way subdivision ensures **locality-aware clustering**. --- ## 2.2 K-D Tree — Operational Decomposition ### 2.2.1 Node Structure ```c struct KDNode { double point[K_DIM]; // K-dimensional point coordinates int splittingDim; // which dimension this node splits on struct KDNode *left; // points with coord < pivot struct KDNode *right; // points with coord >= pivot }; ``` ### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Step 1 — *Root Creation*** - The root is selected by choosing a point (often the **median** along dimension 0) as the pivot, and the entire dataset is partitioned into "left" and "right" subsets based on that pivot. **Step 2 — *Recursive Construction*** - At level $\ell$, the splitting dimension is: $$d = \ell \bmod k$$ - The median of the point set along dimension $d$ becomes the pivot for that subtree. **Step 3 — *Insertion*** - Begin at the root. At each node, compare the new point's $d$-th coordinate with the node's pivot $d$-th coordinate. Go **left** if smaller, **right** otherwise. Descend to the next level and update $d = (d+1) \bmod k$. **Step 4 — *Range Search*** - Recursively traverse the tree. At each node: - If the node's point lies inside the query box, **report it**. - Recurse left and right *only* if the splitting hyperplane intersects the query box. **Step 5 — *Nearest-Neighbor Search (NN)*** - This is the most celebrated application. Algorithm: 1. Traverse down the tree, recording the **best (closest) point so far** with its squared distance $d_{\text{best}}^2$. 2. **Backtrack** up the recursion; for each visited node, compute the perpendicular distance from the query point $q$ to the splitting hyperplane. 3. If that distance $< d_{\text{best}}$, then the **other subtree** may contain a closer point → recurse into it. 4. Update $d_{\text{best}}$ whenever a closer point is found. > [!TIP] > **Why the backtrack is essential:** A point that lies on the "other side" of the splitting hyperplane could still be closer to $q$ than the current best — so we cannot prune naively. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Operation | Quadtree (Avg) | Quadtree (Worst) | K-D Tree (Avg) | K-D Tree (Worst) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Insertion** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | | 2 | **Exact-Match Search** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | | 3 | **Range Search (k-dim)** | $O(n^{1-1/k} + m)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n^{1-1/k} + m)$ | $O(n)$ | | 4 | **Nearest Neighbor** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | | 5 | **Space** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | | 6 | **Height (balanced)** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | | 7 | **Depth (PR-Quadtree, 2D)** | $\log_2 (\text{grid size})$ | — | — | — | > [!WARNING] > In the **range search formula** $O(n^{1-1/k} + m)$: here $k$ is the number of dimensions and **$m$ is the number of reported points** (output-sensitive complexity). This formula is the KTU 2024 expected standard. ### 2.3.1 Key Distance and Pivot Formulas **Squared Euclidean Distance** (used in NN search): $$d^2(q, p) = \sum_{i=1}^{k}(q_i - p_i)^2$$ **Perpendicular distance from query point $q$ to splitting hyperplane at dimension $d$ with pivot value $v$:** $$d_{\perp} = \vert q_d - v \vert$$ **Hyperrectangle-overlap test** (for range search in K-D tree): for a query box $[L_1, H_1] \times [L_2, H_2] \times \cdots \times [L_k, H_k]$ and a node with hyperrectangle $[a_1, b_1] \times \cdots \times [a_k, b_k]$: $$\text{Intersect} \iff \forall i \in [1,k] : \max(a_i, L_i) \leq \min(b_i, H_i)$$ ### 2.3.2 Engineering Utility | Domain | Structure | Why It Is Used | |---|---|---| | **Image Compression (JPEG-2000, GIS)** | Region Quadtree | Exploits uniform pixel blocks | | **Game Development** | Quadtree | Fast 2D collision detection & culling | | **Machine Learning (k-NN)** | K-D Tree | Accelerates nearest-neighbor queries in feature space | | **Computational Geometry** | K-D Tree | Closest-pair of points in $O(n \log n)$ | | **Computer Graphics Ray Tracing** | K-D Tree (of triangles) | Efficient 3D scene intersection | | **Database Indexing** | Quadtree, K-D-B-Tree | Multi-attribute queries | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For Computer Science / IT branches:** K-D trees are heavily tested in k-NN, machine-learning preliminaries, and computational-geometry modules. **For CS-AI specialization:** K-D tree is a *prerequisite* for Ball Tree, VP-Tree, and ANNS algorithms. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Building a Quadtree (PR-Quadtree, capacity = 1) > [!NOTE] > **Setup:** Consider the following 6 2D points to be inserted (capacity = 1, so the root splits as soon as a 2nd point arrives): > **P1 = (10, 40), P2 = (30, 20), P3 = (50, 60), P4 = (70, 30), P5 = (20, 80), P6 = (60, 50)** > Initial root bounds: $[0, 100] \times [0, 100]$, center $(50, 50)$. ### Step-by-Step Insertion Trace **Insert P1 = (10, 40) into root:** - Root is empty → store P1. Root has 1 point. - Tree state: `Root { P1 }` **Insert P2 = (30, 20):** - Root has capacity 1 → **must subdivide**. Subdivide $[0,100]\times[0,100]$ at center $(50,50)$ into 4 quadrants: - **NW**: $[0, 50] \times [50, 100]$ - **NE**: $[50, 100] \times [50, 100]$ - **SW**: $[0, 50] \times [0, 50]$ - **SE**: $[50, 100] \times [0, 50]$ - Re-distribute P1 = (10, 40): $p_x = 10 \leq 50$ and $p_y = 40 < 50$ → **SW** child. - Insert P2 = (30, 20): $p_x = 30 \leq 50$ and $p_y = 20 < 50$ → **SW** child. - P1 and P2 are both in SW → SW must subdivide. **Subdivision of SW** $[0, 50] \times [0, 50]$ at center $(25, 25)$: - **NW-SW**: $[0, 25] \times [25, 50]$ - **NE-SW**: $[25, 50] \times [25, 50]$ - **SW-SW**: $[0, 25] \times [0, 25]$ - **SE-SW**: $[25, 50] \times [0, 25]$ Re-distribute into sub-quadrants: - P1 = (10, 40): $p_x = 10 \leq 25$ and $p_y = 40 \geq 25$ → **NW-SW**. ✓ - P2 = (30, 20): $p_x = 30 > 25$ and $p_y = 20 < 25$ → **SE-SW**. ✓ - No collision; no further subdivision. **Insert P3 = (50, 60):** - Trace from root: $p_x = 50 \leq 50$ (boundary case — take $\leq$) and $p_y = 60 \geq 50$ → **NW** child. - NW is empty → store P3. **Insert P4 = (70, 30):** - Trace from root: $p_x = 70 > 50$ and $p_y = 30 < 50$ → **SE** child. - SE is empty → store P4. **Insert P5 = (20, 80):** - Trace from root: $p_x = 20 \leq 50$ and $p_y = 80 \geq 50$ → **NW** child. - NW already has P3 → must subdivide NW $[0, 50] \times [50, 100]$ at center $(25, 75)$. - Re-distribute: - P3 = (50, 60): $p_x = 50 > 25$ and $p_y = 60 < 75$ → **SE of NW**. - P5 = (20, 80): $p_x = 20 \leq 25$ and $p_y = 80 \geq 75$ → **NW of NW**. - Both in different sub-quadrants → no further split. **Insert P6 = (60, 50):** - Trace from root: $p_x = 60 > 50$ and $p_y = 50 \geq 50$ → **NE** child. - NE is empty → store P6. ### Final Tree Structure (textual representation) ``` Root [0,100]x[0,100] ├── NW [0,50]x[50,100] │ ├── NW-NW [0,25]x[75,100] → {P5 = (20,80)} │ ├── NE-NW [25,50]x[75,100] → empty │ ├── SW-NW [0,25]x[50,75] → empty │ └── SE-NW [25,50]x[50,75] → {P3 = (50,60)} ├── NE [50,100]x[50,100] → {P6 = (60,50)} ├── SW [0,50]x[0,50] │ ├── NW-SW [0,25]x[25,50] → {P1 = (10,40)} │ ├── NE-SW [25,50]x[25,50] → empty │ ├── SW-SW [0,25]x[0,25] → empty │ └── SE-SW [25,50]x[0,25] → {P2 = (30,20)} └── SE [50,100]x[0,50] → {P4 = (70,30)} ``` > [!TIP] > **Range Search Example:** Query: *"Find all points in rectangle $[0, 30] \times [0, 50]$"*. > - Root intersects → descend. **NW** is fully outside (y ≥ 50) → prune. **NE** is fully outside → prune. **SE** is fully outside (x ≥ 50) → prune. **SW** intersects → descend. > - In SW, only **NW-SW** and **SE-SW** are checked. Result: **{P1, P2}**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Building a K-D Tree (2D, k = 2) > [!NOTE] > **Setup:** Points (sorted first by x, then by y): > **(2,3), (5,4), (9,6), (4,7), (8,1), (7,2)** — k = 2. ### Step-by-Step Construction **Step 1 — Root (Level 0, split dim = $0 \bmod 2 = 0$, i.e., x):** - Sort by x: (2,3), (4,7), (5,4), (7,2), (8,1), (9,6) - Median (4th-element of 6, lower median) = **(7, 2)**. Root = (7, 2). - Left subset: {(2,3), (4,7), (5,4)} - Right subset: {(8,1), (9,6)} **Step 2 — Left Subtree (Level 1, split dim = $1 \bmod 2 = 1$, i.e., y):** - Sort {(2,3), (4,7), (5,4)} by y: (2,3), (5,4), (4,7) - Median = **(5, 4)**. Node = (5, 4). - Left-of-(5,4): {(2,3)} - Right-of-(5,4): {(4,7)} **Step 3 — Right Subtree of Root (Level 1, split dim = 1, i.e., y):** - Sort {(8,1), (9,6)} by y: (8,1), (9,6) - Median = **(8, 1)**. Node = (8, 1). - Left-of-(8,1): {} (empty) - Right-of-(8,1): {(9,6)} **Step 4 — Continue to leaves:** - (2,3) becomes a leaf under left-of-(5,4). - (4,7) becomes a leaf under right-of-(5,4). - (9,6) becomes a leaf under right-of-(8,1). ### Final K-D Tree ``` (7, 2) [split: x] / \ (5, 4) (8, 1) [split: y] / \ / \ (2,3) (4,7) null (9,6) [split: x] ``` ### Nearest-Neighbor Search Example > [!NOTE] > **Query:** Find the nearest point to **Q = (6, 5)** in the K-D tree above. **Step 1 — Traverse down:** - Root (7, 2), split on x: $6 < 7$ → go **left** to (5, 4). - (5, 4), split on y: $5 < 4$? No, $5 > 4$ → go **right** to (4, 7). - (4, 7) is a leaf. Compute $d^2 = (6-4)^2 + (5-7)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8$. $d_{\text{best}}^2 = 8$. **Step 2 — Backtrack to (5, 4):** - Distance from Q to splitting hyperplane (y = 4): $|5 - 4| = 1$. Square = 1. - Since $1 < 8$, the left subtree *might* contain a closer point → **recurse left**. - Visit (2, 3): $d^2 = (6-2)^2 + (5-3)^2 = 16 + 4 = 20$. Not better. **Step 3 — Backtrack to root (7, 2):** - Distance from Q to hyperplane (x = 7): $|6 - 7| = 1$. Square = 1. - Since $1 < 8$, right subtree *might* contain a closer point → **recurse right**. - (8, 1) is a leaf: $d^2 = (6-8)^2 + (5-1)^2 = 4 + 16 = 20$. Not better. **Final Answer:** Nearest point to Q = (6, 5) is **(4, 7)** with distance $\sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2}$. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Quadtree | K-D Tree | |---|---|---| | **Splitting Rule** | Geometric midpoint of region | Median of point set along cycling dimension | | **Tree shape** | Unbalanced possible; depends on data clustering | Balanced (with median) | | **Range query pruning** | Easy — quadrant-rectangle intersection | Harder — hyperplane-box intersection | | **NN search efficiency** | Moderate | Excellent (industry gold standard) | | **Implementation complexity** | Low | Moderate | | **Adaptive to data density** | Yes (splits only when full) | Limited (median splitting) | | **Dynamic insert/delete** | Easy | Harder (rebalancing needed) | | **k-D generalization** | Quadtree → Octree (3D), no direct k-D | Native support for any k | | **KTU 2024 exam focus** | Subdivision logic, range query | Construction, NN search algorithm | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Quadtree — Hierarchical Block Architecture The following Mermaid diagram represents the **block-level functional architecture** of a PR-Quadtree with capacity = 1, after inserting the 6 points from Worked Example 1. ```mermaid graph TD A["Root Node
Bounds 0,100 x 0,100
Capacity 1"] A --> B["NW Child
0,50 x 50,100"] A --> C["NE Child
50,100 x 50,100
Holds P6 60,50"] A --> D["SW Child
0,50 x 0,50"] A --> E["SE Child
50,100 x 0,50
Holds P4 70,30"] B --> F["NW-NW
0,25 x 75,100
Holds P5 20,80"] B --> G["NE-NW
25,50 x 75,100
Empty"] B --> H["SW-NW
0,25 x 50,75
Empty"] B --> I["SE-NW
25,50 x 50,75
Holds P3 50,60"] D --> J["NW-SW
0,25 x 25,50
Holds P1 10,40"] D --> K["NE-SW
25,50 x 25,50
Empty"] D --> L["SW-SW
0,25 x 0,25
Empty"] D --> M["SE-SW
25,50 x 0,25
Holds P2 30,20"] ``` ### 4.1.1 Quadtree Quadrant Naming Convention Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR Q["Splitting Center cx,cy"] --> Q1["NW
x less than cx
y greater than cy"] Q --> Q2["NE
x greater than cx
y greater than cy"] Q --> Q3["SW
x less than cx
y less than cy"] Q --> Q4["SE
x greater than cx
y less than cy"] ``` --- ## 4.2 K-D Tree — Construction & NN-Search Architecture The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the K-D tree from Worked Example 2, with **splitting dimensions annotated** at each level. ```mermaid graph TD R["Root 7,2
Split Dim x equal 0"] R --> L["Node 5,4
Split Dim y equal 1"] R --> Rt["Node 8,1
Split Dim y equal 1"] L --> LL["Leaf 2,3
Split Dim x equal 0"] L --> LR["Leaf 4,7
Split Dim x equal 0"] Rt --> RtL["null
No left child"] Rt --> RtR["Leaf 9,6
Split Dim x equal 0"] ``` ### 4.2.1 K-D Tree Nearest-Neighbor Search Flow ```mermaid graph TD S0["Start at root node"] S0 --> S1["Recurse down using splitting dimension rule
q_i less than pivot_i go left else right"] S1 --> S2["Compute distance from Q to current leaf
Update d_best squared"] S2 --> S3["Backtrack to parent"] S3 --> S4{"Is perpendicular distance squared
less than d_best?"} S4 -- Yes --> S5["Recurse into opposite subtree
Update d_best if closer point found"] S4 -- No --> S6["Prune this subtree
No closer point possible"] S5 --> S7{"More ancestors to check?"} S6 --> S7 S7 -- Yes --> S3 S7 -- No --> S8["Return current best point as NN"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Comparative Block Architecture: Quadtree vs. K-D Tree ```mermaid graph TB subgraph QUADTREE Q1["Space Partitioning"] --> Q2["Equal Quadrant Split"] Q2 --> Q3["4 Children per Node"] Q3 --> Q4["Region-based Storage"] end subgraph KDTREE K1["Data Partitioning"] --> K2["Median-based Split"] K2 --> K3["2 Children per Node"] K3 --> K4["Point-based Storage"] end Q4 --> APPS1["Applications: Image Compression
2D Collision Detection
Spatial Indexing"] K4 --> APPS2["Applications: k-NN Search
ML Feature Indexing
Ray Tracing
Closest-Pair Problems"] ``` > [!TIP] > **Block-Level Insight:** Notice the *fundamental architectural difference* — Quadtree's branching factor scales **with the dimension** (4 in 2D, 8 in 3D), whereas K-D tree's branching factor is *always 2*, regardless of $k$. This is why K-D trees scale gracefully to high dimensions while Quadtrees do not (the term *Octree* = 3D Quadtree; no clean generalization to 4D exists). # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** > **Q:** Define a *Point Quadtree*. With a neat diagram, explain how insertion of a 2D point takes place in a Point Quadtree. State one key application. #### Model Answer (3 marks): **Definition (1 mark):** A *Point Quadtree* is a hierarchical spatial data structure in which every internal node contains exactly one 2D point and has up to four children (NW, NE, SW, SE). A new point is inserted by recursive comparison of x and y coordinates, descending into the appropriate quadrant. **Insertion Logic (1 mark):** - Compare incoming point $P = (p_x, p_y)$ with the current node's point $N = (n_x, n_y)$. - If $P \neq N$ and node is leaf → store $P$ as new child in the quadrant determined by sign of $(p_x - n_x)$ and $(p_y - n_y)$. - Descend recursively until an empty leaf is found. **Application (1 mark):** Used in **2D spatial indexing** for range queries and **nearest-neighbor search in geographic information systems (GIS)**. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** > **Q:** What is a *K-D Tree*? How does the splitting dimension change with depth for a k-dimensional K-D tree? #### Model Answer (3 marks): **Definition (2 marks):** A *K-Dimensional Tree* (K-D Tree) is a generalization of the Binary Search Tree to **k-dimensional** data. Each node stores a k-dimensional point and splits the data into two halves along one specific dimension, chosen cyclically with the depth of the node. **Splitting Dimension (1 mark):** At a node located at depth $\ell$ (with the root at $\ell = 0$), the splitting dimension is computed as: $$d = \ell \bmod k$$ For example, with $k = 3$: at $\ell = 0$ split on dim 0, at $\ell = 1$ split on dim 1, at $\ell = 2$ split on dim 2, at $\ell = 3$ split on dim 0 again, and so on (cyclically). --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) forgetting to **show the cyclically-changing splitting dimension** at each K-D tree level, (2) failing to **backtrack** in the nearest-neighbor search and reporting only the leaf, (3) confusing *quadrant* assignment in Quadtree due to **Y-axis orientation** mismatch, and (4) **not annotating the complexity** of each operation. Always state the **average-case and worst-case** complexity explicitly. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Quadtree Focus **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q8 (a) + (b)]** > **(a)** Construct a **PR-Quadtree** (Point-Region, capacity = 1) for the following 5 points: (12, 35), (45, 60), (28, 18), (55, 42), (8, 75). Assume the initial space is $[0, 64] \times [0, 64]$ and the center is (32, 32). Show every step. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Using the tree constructed in (a), perform a **range search** for the rectangle $[20, 50] \times [10, 50]$. Show all subtrees visited, points reported, and final answer. State the time complexity. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Step-by-Step Construction (7 marks):** **Insert (12, 35) into Root $[0,64] \times [0,64]$:** - Root empty → store. Root holds 1 point. **Insert (45, 60):** - Root full (cap = 1) → subdivide at center (32, 32). - 4 quadrants: NW $[0,32]\times[32,64]$, NE $[32,64]\times[32,64]$, SW $[0,32]\times[0,32]$, SE $[32,64]\times[0,32]$. - Re-distribute (12, 35): $12 \leq 32$ and $35 \geq 32$ → **NW**. ✓ - Insert (45, 60): $45 > 32$ and $60 \geq 32$ → **NE**. ✓ **Insert (28, 18):** - Root intersects → descend. NW holds (12,35); NE holds (45,60). SW and SE empty. - $28 \leq 32$ and $18 < 32$ → **SW**. SW is empty → store. **Insert (55, 42):** - $55 > 32$ and $42 \geq 32$ → **NE**. NE holds (45, 60) → must subdivide NE. - Subdivide NE $[32,64] \times [32,64]$ at center (48, 48): - NW-NE: $[32,48] \times [48,64]$ - NE-NE: $[48,64] \times [48,64]$ - SW-NE: $[32,48] \times [32,48]$ - SE-NE: $[48,64] \times [32,48]$ - Re-distribute (45, 60): $45 \leq 48$ and $60 \geq 48$ → **NW-NE**. - Insert (55, 42): $55 > 48$ and $42 < 48$ → **SE-NE**. **Insert (8, 75):** - $8 \leq 32$ and $75 \geq 32$ → **NW**. NW holds (12, 35) → must subdivide NW. - Subdivide NW $[0,32] \times [32,64]$ at center (16, 48): - NW-NW: $[0,16] \times [48,64]$ - NE-NW: $[16,32] \times [48,64]$ - SW-NW: $[0,16] \times [32,48]$ - SE-NW: $[16,32] \times [32,48]$ - Re-distribute (12, 35): $12 \leq 16$ and $35 < 48$ → **SW-NW**. - Insert (8, 75): $8 \leq 16$ and $75 \geq 48$ → **NW-NW**. **Final PR-Quadtree:** ``` Root [0,64]x[0,64] ├── NW [0,32]x[32,64] │ ├── NW-NW [0,16]x[48,64] → {(8, 75)} │ ├── NE-NW [16,32]x[48,64] → empty │ ├── SW-NW [0,16]x[32,48] → {(12, 35)} │ └── SE-NW [16,32]x[32,48] → empty ├── NE [32,64]x[32,64] │ ├── NW-NE [32,48]x[48,64] → {(45, 60)} │ ├── NE-NE [48,64]x[48,64] → empty │ ├── SW-NE [32,48]x[32,48] → empty │ └── SE-NE [48,64]x[32,48] → {(55, 42)} ├── SW [0,32]x[0,32] → {(28, 18)} └── SE [32,64]x[0,32] → empty ``` **(b) Range Search $[20, 50] \times [10, 50]$ (7 marks):** - **Root** $[0,64]\times[0,64]$ → fully contains query → must descend into all overlapping children. - **NW** $[0,32]\times[32,64]$: overlaps query ($y$-range [10,50] intersects [32,64]) → descend. - NW-NW $[0,16]\times[48,64]$: $x$-range $[0,16]$ does NOT overlap $[20,50]$ → **prune**. ✗ - NE-NW $[16,32]\times[48,64]$: $x$-range $[16,32]$ overlaps; $y$-range $[48,64]$ does NOT overlap $[10,50]$ → **prune**. ✗ - SW-NW $[0,16]\times[32,48]$: $x$-range $[0,16]$ does NOT overlap $[20,50]$ → **prune**. ✗ - SE-NW $[16,32]\times[32,48]$: $x$-range $[16,32]$ overlaps; $y$-range $[32,48]$ overlaps $[10,50]$ → **descend**. Point (12, 35) not in this node — **empty**. Move on. - **NE** $[32,64]\times[32,64]$: $x$-range $[32,64]$ overlaps $[20,50]$; $y$-range $[32,64]$ overlaps $[10,50]$ → descend. - NW-NE $[32,48]\times[48,64]$: $y$-range $[48,64]$ does NOT overlap → **prune**. ✗ - NE-NE $[48,64]\times[48,64]$: $x$ does not overlap ($48 > 50$? No, $48 \leq 50$ OK, $64 > 50$ OK). Wait: query is $[20,50]$. $48 \in [20,50]$ ✓. $y$-range $[48,64]$ does NOT overlap $[10,50]$ → **prune**. ✗ - SW-NE $[32,48]\times[32,48]$: $x$ overlaps ($32-48$ in $[20,50]$) ✓. $y$ overlaps ($32-48$ in $[10,50]$) ✓. **Empty node**. - SE-NE $[48,64]\times[32,48]$: $x$ overlaps ($48-64$, but upper bound $64 > 50$? Overlap range is $[48,50]$) ✓. $y$ overlaps ($32-48$ in $[10,50]$) ✓. **Point (55, 42): is it inside query?** $x=55 > 50$ → **NO, not reported**. - **SW** $[0,32]\times[0,32]$: $x$-range $[0,32]$ overlaps $[20,50]$ ($20-32$ overlap). $y$-range $[0,32]$ overlaps $[10,50]$ ($10-32$ overlap) → descend. **Point (28, 18): is it inside query?** $x = 28 \in [20,50]$ ✓, $y = 18 \in [10,50]$ ✓ → **REPORTED**. ✓ - **SE** $[32,64]\times[0,32]$: $x$-range $[32,64]$ overlaps $[20,50]$. $y$-range $[0,32]$ overlaps $[10,50]$. → descend. **Empty node**. **Final Reported Set:** { **(28, 18)** } **Time Complexity (1 mark):** $O(\sqrt{n} + m)$ in 2D Quadtree range search, where $m$ = number of reported points (here $m = 1$). Average $O(\log n)$ for point queries; worst case $O(n)$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — K-D Tree Focus **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q9 (a) + (b)]** > **(a)** Construct a 2-D K-D Tree (k = 2) for the following 2D points: (3, 6), (5, 4), (2, 7), (8, 1), (9, 3), (7, 2). Use the median-based construction. At each level, indicate the splitting dimension explicitly. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Using the K-D tree built in (a), find the **nearest neighbor** to the query point **Q = (6, 5)**. Show every step including backtracking. Also report the nearest distance. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Step-by-Step Construction (7 marks):** **Level 0 (split dim = x, dim 0):** - Points: (3,6), (5,4), (2,7), (8,1), (9,3), (7,2) - Sort by x: (2,7), (3,6), (5,4), (7,2), (8,1), (9,3) - Median (lower median, 3rd element of 6) = **(5, 4)**. → **Root = (5, 4)**, split dim = **x**. - Left of (5,4) by x: {(2,7), (3,6)} (smaller x) - Right of (5,4) by x: {(7,2), (8,1), (9,3)} (larger x) **Level 1, Left Subtree (split dim = y, dim 1):** - Points: (2,7), (3,6). Sort by y: (3,6), (2,7). - Median = **(3, 6)**. → Node = **(3, 6)**, split dim = **y**. - Left of (3,6) by y: {(2,7)} (smaller y, i.e., y < 6? Wait, (2,7) has y=7 > 6, so it goes to right). Let me redo the splitting rule clearly. At each node with split dimension $d$: - Points with $p_d < \text{pivot}_d$ → left subtree. - Points with $p_d \geq \text{pivot}_d$ → right subtree. - Points: (2,7), (3,6). Pivot = (3,6), split dim = y. - (2,7): y = 7 ≥ 6 → **right** subtree of (3,6). - (3,6) itself: removed (used as pivot). - Right subtree has {(2,7)} → becomes a leaf. **Level 1, Right Subtree of Root (split dim = y, dim 1):** - Points: (7,2), (8,1), (9,3). Sort by y: (8,1), (7,2), (9,3). - Median = **(7, 2)**. → Node = **(7, 2)**, split dim = **y**. - (8,1): y = 1 < 2 → **left** subtree. - (9,3): y = 3 ≥ 2 → **right** subtree. **Level 2, Right-Left Subtree of Root (split dim = x, dim 0):** - Points: {(8,1)}. → becomes a leaf. **Level 2, Right-Right Subtree of Root (split dim = x, dim 0):** - Points: {(9,3)}. → becomes a leaf. **Final 2-D K-D Tree:** ``` (5, 4) [Level 0, split: x] / \ (3, 6) (7, 2) [Level 1, split: y] / \ / \ null (2,7) (8,1) (9,3) [Level 2, split: x] ``` **(b) Nearest Neighbor Search for Q = (6, 5) (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Descend to leaf:** - **Root (5, 4)**, split dim = x: $q_x = 6 \geq 5$ → go **right** to (7, 2). $d^2(6,5; 5,4) = 1 + 1 = 2$. - **Node (7, 2)**, split dim = y: $q_y = 5 \geq 2$ → go **right** to (9, 3). $d^2(6,5; 7,2) = 1 + 9 = 10$. - **Leaf (9, 3)**, split dim = x: $q_x = 6 < 9$ → go **left** (null). Report distance $d^2(6,5; 9,3) = 9 + 4 = 13$. - **Current best:** point (9, 3), $d_{\text{best}}^2 = 13$. **Step 2 — Backtrack to (7, 2):** - Compute perpendicular distance to splitting hyperplane (y = 2): $|q_y - 2| = |5 - 2| = 3$. Squared = 9. - Since $9 < 13$ → **recurse into opposite subtree (left)** of (7, 2). - Visit (8, 1): $d^2(6,5; 8,1) = 4 + 16 = 20$. Not better. - $d_{\text{best}}^2$ remains 13. **Step 3 — Backtrack to root (5, 4):** - Compute perpendicular distance to splitting hyperplane (x = 5): $|q_x - 5| = |6 - 5| = 1$. Squared = 1. - Since $1 < 13$ → **recurse into opposite subtree (left)** of root (5, 4). - Visit (3, 6): $d^2(6,5; 3,6) = 9 + 1 = 10$. **New best!** $d_{\text{best}}^2 = 10$. - Recurse left of (3, 6): null. **Step 4 — Continue backtracking:** - Backtrack from (3, 6) to root. Perpendicular distance to (3,6)'s hyperplane (y = 6): $|5 - 6| = 1$, squared = 1 < 10 → check opposite subtree (left of (3,6)): null. **Final Answer (2 marks):** - **Nearest Neighbor: (3, 6)** - **Nearest Distance:** $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$ **Time Complexity (1 mark):** Average $O(\log n)$, worst-case $O(n)$ for highly skewed point distributions. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use this as your **last-10-minute KTU exam revision** checklist. - [ ] **Quadtree = 4-ary spatial tree** (2D). **Octree = 8-ary** (3D). K-D tree = **binary** but k-dim. - [ ] Quadtree **subdivides space** at geometric midpoints. K-D tree **subdivides data** at medians. - [ ] Quadtree split axis is **always x AND y** (parallel to coordinate axes, in 2D). K-D tree cycles dimensions: $d = \ell \bmod k$. - [ ] **Capacity parameter** $b$ in PR-Quadtree controls when subdivision occurs (default $b = 1$). - [ ] Average complexity for all ops: **O(log n)**. Worst case: **O(n)** (skewed data). - [ ] **Range search** in K-D tree: $O(n^{1-1/k} + m)$, where $m$ is the number of points reported. - [ ] **Nearest-neighbor** in K-D tree requires **backtracking**; the perpendicular distance test decides whether to recurse. - [ ] **Inventor reminders (frequently asked in 1-mark KTU questions):** - Quadtree: **Finkel & Bentley (1974)** - K-D Tree: **Jon Bentley (1975)** - [ ] **Quadtree applications:** image compression, GIS, 2D collision detection, culling in game engines. - [ ] **K-D tree applications:** k-NN in ML, ray tracing in CG, closest-pair of points, k-means clustering initialization. - [ ] **Y-axis convention:** For Quadtree quadrant assignment, *clearly state* whether Y is up (graphics) or Y is down (math). Mismatches cost 0.5–1 mark. - [ ] **Median choice:** Lower median vs. upper median. KTU expects students to declare convention and stick to it. - [ ] **For deletion in K-D tree:** Either mark the node "deleted" lazily, or find a replacement using the *find-min* operation on the deletion dimension. - [ ] **For deletion in Quadtree:** Optionally merge sibling subtrees whose total point count ≤ capacity. - [ ] **Failure mode to remember:** When building a *balanced* K-D tree, use **median**, not arbitrary. Otherwise the tree becomes skewed → worst-case O(n). - [ ] **Variants often asked:** - PR-Quadtree vs. Point Quadtree vs. MX-Quadtree vs. Region Quadtree. - K-D tree vs. K-D-B-Tree (disk-based) vs. Ball Tree (high-dim). - [ ] **Final high-yield comparison line for KTU 2-mark question:** *"Quadtree is space-partitioning, region-aware, with branching factor 4; K-D tree is data-partitioning, point-aware, binary, and dimension-cycling."*

topics.title: R-Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST495 - ADVANCED DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** R-Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: R-TREES — SPATIAL INDEXING FOUNDATIONS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Guttman, 1984):**
> An **R-Tree** is a hierarchical, height-balanced spatial index structure that organizes *d*-dimensional spatial objects (typically axis-aligned Minimum Bounding Rectangles — MBRs) by grouping nearby objects and representing each group by its **Minimum Bounding Rectangle (MBR)** at the next higher level. It is the spatial generalization of the B+-Tree.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Shelf" Intuition

Imagine a massive library with thousands of books scattered across many rooms. A librarian cannot search room-by-room. Instead, she maintains **floor plans** (MBRs):

- Each **book** (leaf entry) sits on a shelf — its location is a *point* or a *small rectangle*.
- Each **shelf** has a *label* that is a rectangle enclosing every book on it.
- Each **aisle** has a *label* — a rectangle enclosing every shelf.
- Each **wing/floor** has a *label* — a rectangle enclosing every aisle.

Now, to find a book, the librarian first checks the floor plan of each wing, then each aisle, then each shelf. This is **exactly** how an R-Tree works in 2D/3D space — it indexes rectangles inside rectangles inside rectangles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The R-Tree is a **disk-based** (external memory) structure, meaning it is engineered to minimize disk I/O by maximizing the number of entries per node (fan-out), just like a B+-Tree.

### 1.2 Structural Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|---|---|
| **MBR (Minimum Bounding Rectangle)** | The smallest axis-aligned rectangle that completely contains a set of spatial objects. |
| **Leaf Node** | Contains index entries of the form **(I, object-id)** where *I* is an MBR and *object-id* points to the actual spatial object. |
| **Internal Node** | Contains entries of the form **(I, child-pointer)** where *I* is the MBR that minimally bounds all MBRs in the child node. |
| **Fan-out (M, m)** | Maximum (*M*) and minimum (*m*) number of entries per node. Invariant: $m \le \lceil M/2 \rceil$. |
| **Height (h)** | The number of levels from root to leaf. R-Tree is height-balanced: all leaves are at the same level. |

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Minimum entries per node:** $m = \lceil M/2 \rceil$ (for standard R-Tree).
- **Page size:** Typically **4 KB** or **8 KB** (matches disk block).
- **Dimensionality:** R-Trees are defined for arbitrary $d$, but practical use is in **2D** (GIS) and **3D** (CAD/medical).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 R-Tree Properties (Invariants)

A valid R-Tree of order $(M, m)$ must satisfy **five** structural properties:

1. **Every leaf node** contains between $m$ and $M$ index records, unless it is the root.
2. **Every internal node** (except root) contains between $m$ and $M$ children.
3. **The root** has at least 2 children (unless it is a leaf).
4. **All leaves** appear at the same depth ⇒ **height-balanced**.
5. **The MBR of a non-leaf entry** is the smallest rectangle that spatially contains the MBRs of all entries in its child node.

## 2.2 Core Operations

### A. Search (Window Query)
Given a query rectangle $Q$, find all data rectangles intersecting $Q$.

**Algorithm (Recursive):**
- At root, examine every entry. If `entry.MBR` intersects $Q$, recurse into the child.
- At leaf, test every object against $Q$; return matches.

### B. Insert
1. **ChooseLeaf** — descend recursively, picking at each level the child whose MBR requires **least enlargement** to contain the new entry (ties broken by smallest area).
2. **Add** the entry to the chosen leaf.
3. If **overflow** (entries > $M$), invoke **SplitNode** (linear, quadratic, or exponential cost).
4. Propagate splits upward; if the root splits, create a new root (tree grows by 1 level).

### C. SplitNode — Quadratic Split (Most-Examined Variant)

**Cost function:** Pick two seeds as the pair that would waste the **most area** if put in the same group.

$$ \text{Waste}(E_i, E_j) = \text{Area}(\text{MBR}(E_i \cup E_j)) - \text{Area}(E_i) - \text{Area}(E_j) $$

The remaining entries are assigned one by one to the group where the MBR enlargement is smallest. Final step ensures both groups contain at least $m$ entries.

### D. Delete
1. **FindLeaf** — locate the leaf containing the entry.
2. Remove the entry. If leaf underflows (< $m$), the node is **eliminated** and its orphans are re-inserted at the same level.
3. **CondenseTree** — propagate deletion upward, removing under-full nodes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all exam-relevant formulas** for R-Tree operations. Memorize them — they appear almost every KTU exam cycle.

| Concept | Formula / Rule |
|---|---|
| **Node capacity bounds** | $m \le \lceil M/2 \rceil$ |
| **Tree height** | $h = 1 + \lceil \log_m N \rceil$ for $N$ objects |
| **MBR Area (2D)** | $\text{Area} = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) \cdot (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$ |
| **MBR Area (d-Dim)** | $\text{Area} = \prod_{k=1}^{d}(u_k - l_k)$ |
| **MBR Overlap Test (2D)** | Rectangles $A,B$ overlap iff $A.l_x \le B.u_x$ AND $A.u_x \ge B.l_x$ AND $A.l_y \le B.u_y$ AND $A.u_y \ge B.l_y$ |
| **MBR Containment Test** | $A \subseteq B$ iff $B.l_x \le A.l_x$ AND $A.u_x \le B.u_x$ AND $B.l_y \le A.l_y$ AND $A.u_y \le B.u_y$ |
| **Enlargement Area (Insert)** | $\Delta\text{Area} = \text{Area}(\text{MBR}(E \cup C_i)) - \text{Area}(C_i)$ |
| **Quadratic Split — PickSeeds** | Maximize $W_{ij} = \text{Area}(\text{MBR}(E_i,E_j)) - \text{Area}(E_i) - \text{Area}(E_j)$ |
| **Quadratic Split — PickNext** | Assign to group $G$ maximizing $d_1 - d_2$, where $d_k = \text{Area enlargement in } G_k$ |
| **Maximum leaf entries** | $M$ (page-size dependent) |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes (`|`) were used in the table above** to prevent markdown breaking. All "such that" or "divides" relations are written as plain text or math symbols.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Geographic Information Systems (GIS):** Google Maps, OpenStreetMap use R-Tree variants (R+-Tree, R\*-Tree) to find restaurants/hotels inside a viewport.
- **CAD/CAM systems:** Indexing 2D/3D design components.
- **Computer vision:** Indexing image feature descriptors in SIFT/SURF pipelines.
- **Multi-dimensional databases (OLAP):** Indexing cube cells.
- **Network design:** Routing in VLSI chip layout.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Constructing an R-Tree

**Problem (KTU-style):** Given $M = 4$ (max entries per node), insert the following 2D rectangles one by one and show the final tree:

$$ R_1 = [0,0,2,2], \quad R_2 = [3,1,5,3], \quad R_3 = [1,4,3,6], \quad R_4 = [4,5,6,7] $$

Format: $[x_{low}, y_{low}, x_{high}, y_{high}]$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Insert $R_1$:**
- Tree has no root. Create root leaf containing $R_1$.
- Root MBR: $[0,0,2,2]$.

**Insert $R_2 = [3,1,5,3]$:**
- Root has 1 entry (no choice). Add to root.
- Node count = 2 (no overflow, $M=4$).
- Root MBR now spans both: $\text{MBR} = [0,0,5,3]$.

**Insert $R_3 = [1,4,3,6]$:**
- Root has 2 entries; check enlargement for each.
  - Enlargement if added to $R_1$'s group: new MBR = $[0,0,3,6]$, old = $[0,0,2,2]$. Old area $= 4$, new area $= 18$. $\Delta = 14$.
  - Enlargement if added to $R_2$'s group: new MBR = $[3,1,5,6]$, old = $[3,1,5,3]$. Old area $= 6$, new area $= 15$. $\Delta = 9$.
- **Choose $R_2$'s group** (smaller enlargement = 9).
- Root now contains $R_1, R_2, R_3$. No overflow.

**Insert $R_4 = [4,5,6,7]$:**
- Compute enlargements:
  - With $R_1$: new MBR $[0,0,6,7]$, area $= 42$, $\Delta = 38$.
  - With $R_2$: new MBR $[3,1,6,7]$, area $= 24$, $\Delta = 18$.
  - With $R_3$: new MBR $[1,4,6,7]$, area $= 15$, old area $= 6$, $\Delta = 9$.
- **Choose $R_3$'s group** ($\Delta = 9$).
- Root now contains $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$. **Exactly 4 entries — still no overflow.**

**Final Tree (single root leaf):**

```
ROOT (Leaf)
 ├── R1 = [0,0,2,2]
 ├── R2 = [3,1,5,3]
 ├── R3 = [1,4,3,6]
 └── R4 = [4,5,6,7]
```

Root MBR = $[0,0,6,7]$, area = $42$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Quadratic Split (KTU's Favorite Question)

**Problem:** Given $M = 4$ and a node overflowing with 5 entries, perform the **Quadratic Split**:

$$ E_1=[0,0,2,2], \; E_2=[3,1,5,3], \; E_3=[1,4,3,6], \; E_4=[4,5,6,7], \; E_5=[5,0,7,2] $$

### Step 1: PickSeeds (find worst pair)

Compute $W_{ij} = \text{Area}(\text{MBR}(E_i,E_j)) - \text{Area}(E_i) - \text{Area}(E_j)$ for all pairs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{1,2} &= \text{Area}([0,0,5,3]) - 4 - 6 = 15 - 10 = 5 \\
W_{1,3} &= \text{Area}([0,0,3,6]) - 4 - 6 = 18 - 10 = 8 \\
W_{1,4} &= \text{Area}([0,0,6,7]) - 4 - 4 = 42 - 8 = 34 \\
W_{1,5} &= \text{Area}([0,0,7,2]) - 4 - 4 = 14 - 8 = 6 \\
W_{2,3} &= \text{Area}([1,1,5,6]) - 6 - 6 = 20 - 12 = 8 \\
W_{2,4} &= \text{Area}([3,1,6,7]) - 6 - 4 = 24 - 10 = 14 \\
W_{2,5} &= \text{Area}([3,0,7,3]) - 6 - 4 = 16 - 10 = 6 \\
W_{3,4} &= \text{Area}([1,4,6,7]) - 6 - 4 = 15 - 10 = 5 \\
W_{3,5} &= \text{Area}([1,0,7,6]) - 6 - 4 = 42 - 10 = 32 \\
W_{4,5} &= \text{Area}([4,0,7,7]) - 4 - 4 = 21 - 8 = 13 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximum waste = 34, achieved by $(E_1, E_4)$** ⇒ Seeds = $E_1, E_4$.

### Step 2: Form initial groups

- **Group A:** $E_1 = [0,0,2,2]$, MBR_A = $[0,0,2,2]$, area = 4.
- **Group B:** $E_4 = [4,5,6,7]$, MBR_B = $[4,5,6,7]$, area = 4.

### Step 3: PickNext for $E_2, E_3, E_5$

For each remaining entry, compute $d_1 = $ enlargement in A, $d_2 = $ enlargement in B. Assign to the group with smaller enlargement. Choose the entry with **maximum preference** $|d_1 - d_2|$ first.

**Entry $E_2 = [3,1,5,3]$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MBR}(A \cup E_2) &= [0,0,5,3], \text{ area} = 15 \Rightarrow d_1 = 15 - 4 = 11 \\
\text{MBR}(B \cup E_2) &= [3,1,6,7], \text{ area} = 24 \Rightarrow d_2 = 24 - 4 = 20 \\
\text{Preference} &= \vert 11 - 20 \vert = 9 \text{ (prefer A)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry $E_3 = [1,4,3,6]$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MBR}(A \cup E_3) &= [0,0,3,6], \text{ area} = 18 \Rightarrow d_1 = 18 - 4 = 14 \\
\text{MBR}(B \cup E_3) &= [1,4,6,7], \text{ area} = 15 \Rightarrow d_2 = 15 - 4 = 11 \\
\text{Preference} &= \vert 14 - 11 \vert = 3 \text{ (prefer B)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry $E_5 = [5,0,7,2]$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MBR}(A \cup E_5) &= [0,0,7,2], \text{ area} = 14 \Rightarrow d_1 = 14 - 4 = 10 \\
\text{MBR}(B \cup E_5) &= [4,0,7,7], \text{ area} = 21 \Rightarrow d_2 = 21 - 4 = 17 \\
\text{Preference} &= \vert 10 - 17 \vert = 7 \text{ (prefer A)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Assign in preference order

1. **$E_2$** (pref=9, choose A) → A = $\{E_1, E_2\}$, MBR_A = $[0,0,5,3]$, area = 15.
2. **$E_5$** (pref=7, choose A) → A = $\{E_1, E_2, E_5\}$, MBR_A = $[0,0,7,3]$, area = 21.
3. **$E_3$** (pref=3, choose B) → B = $\{E_4, E_3\}$, MBR_B = $[1,4,6,7]$, area = 15.

**Final Groups:**
- **Node A:** $E_1, E_2, E_5$ — 3 entries (≥ $m$ where $m=2$) ✓
- **Node B:** $E_3, E_4$ — 2 entries (≥ $m$) ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation check (KTU Examiner's Trick):** For $M=4$, $m = \lceil M/2 \rceil = 2$. Both groups satisfy $2 \le$ entries $\le 4$. The split is valid.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Search (Window Query)

**Problem:** Using the tree from Example 1, find all rectangles intersecting the query window $Q = [2,1,4,5]$.

### Recursive Search Steps

1. **Visit Root:** Test root MBR $[0,0,6,7]$ against $Q=[2,1,4,5]$.
   - Overlap test: $[0,0,6,7]$ vs $[2,1,4,5]$ → clearly overlap. Recurse.

2. **Examine each child entry:**
   - $R_1 = [0,0,2,2]$: Overlap test: $0 \le 4$ ✓, $2 \ge 2$ ✓, $0 \le 5$ ✓, $2 \ge 1$ ✓ → **OVERLAPS** ✓
   - $R_2 = [3,1,5,3]$: $0 \le 5$ ✓, $5 \ge 2$ ✓, $0 \le 5$ ✓, $3 \ge 1$ ✓ → **OVERLAPS** ✓
   - $R_3 = [1,4,3,6]$: $0 \le 4$ ✓, $3 \ge 2$ ✓, $0 \le 6$ ✓, $6 \ge 1$ ✓ → **OVERLAPS** ✓
   - $R_4 = [4,5,6,7]$: $0 \le 6$ ✓, $6 \ge 2$ ✓, $0 \le 7$ ✓, $7 \ge 1$ ✓ → **OVERLAPS** ✓

3. **Result:** All four rectangles $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ are reported as intersecting $Q$.

> [!NOTE]
> In larger trees, the strength of R-Tree search is that an entire sub-tree can be **pruned** whenever its covering MBR does not overlap $Q$. This is the source of its efficiency.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Key Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| Search | $O(\log_m N)$ average; $O(N)$ worst | $O(N)$ | MBR overlap with query |
| Insert (no split) | $O(\log_m N)$ | — | MBR enlargement minimization |
| Insert (with split) | $O(M \log M)$ | — | Node overflow > $M$ |
| Delete | $O(\log_m N)$ | — | Node underflow < $m$ |
| Range Query | $O(\log_m N + k)$ | — | $k$ = number of results |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 R-Tree Hierarchical Architecture (2D Example)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates a fully constructed R-Tree with $M = 4$ and a height of $h = 2$ after multiple inserts and one split.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode["ROOT NODE\nMBR: 0,0 to 10,8\nEntries: 2"]
    childA["INTERNAL NODE A\nMBR: 0,0 to 5,5\nEntries: 2"]
    childB["INTERNAL NODE B\nMBR: 5,2 to 10,8\nEntries: 2"]
    leafA1["LEAF A1\nMBR: 0,0 to 2,2\nR1, R2"]
    leafA2["LEAF A2\nMBR: 3,1 to 5,5\nR3, R4"]
    leafB1["LEAF B1\nMBR: 5,2 to 7,4\nR5, R6"]
    leafB2["LEAF B2\nMBR: 7,5 to 10,8\nR7, R8"]
    rootNode --> childA
    rootNode --> childB
    childA --> leafA1
    childA --> leafA2
    childB --> leafB1
    childB --> leafB2
```

**Reading the diagram:** The root covers the *entire* dataset via its MBR. Each internal node's MBR encloses the MBRs of its children. Each leaf's MBR encloses the actual data rectangles. Notice that leaves at the same level may still have **overlapping MBRs** — this is a defining (and sometimes problematic) property of standard R-Trees.

## 4.2 R-Tree Search Flow (Window Query)

```mermaid
graph TD
    start(["Start: Query Q arrives"]) --> visitRoot["Visit Root Node\nRead MBR of each entry"]
    visitRoot --> checkOverlap{"Does entry MBR\noverlap Q?"}
    checkOverlap -- Yes --> recurseChild["Recurse into child node\nDisk I/O"]
    checkOverlap -- No --> pruneSub["PRUNE entire subtree\nSkip - no result possible"]
    recurseChild --> isLeaf{"Is child a leaf?"}
    isLeaf -- No --> checkOverlap
    isLeaf -- Yes --> testObjects["Test each object\nin leaf against Q"]
    testObjects --> report["Report all\nintersecting objects"]
    pruneSub --> nextEntry{"More entries?"}
    report --> nextEntry
    nextEntry -- Yes --> checkOverlap
    nextEntry -- No --> endNode(["End: Return results"])
```

**Interpretation:** This is the algorithmic "decision tree" for a window query. The `PRUNE` branch is the *efficiency mechanism* of R-Trees — when an MBR fails the overlap test, an entire subtree of potentially millions of objects is eliminated with one MBR comparison.

## 4.3 Insert Operation Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    insertStart(["Insert Rectangle R"]) --> chooseLeaf["ChooseLeaf phase\nDescend tree picking child\nwith MIN MBR enlargement"]
    chooseLeaf --> reachedLeaf["Reached leaf node L"]
    reachedLeaf --> addEntry["Add R to L"]
    addEntry --> overflow{"L has more\nthan M entries?"}
    overflow -- No --> adjustMBRs["Adjust MBRs along\npath to root\nInsert complete"]
    overflow -- Yes --> splitNode["SplitNode phase\nQuadratic algorithm\nTwo new nodes L1, L2"]
    splitNode --> propagateSplit["Propagate split upward\nAdjust MBRs of ancestors"]
    propagateSplit --> rootSplit{"Did root split?"}
    rootSplit -- Yes --> newRoot["Create NEW ROOT\nTree height increases by 1\nInsert complete"]
    rootSplit -- No --> adjustMBRs
```

## 4.4 Quadratic Split Sub-Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    splitStart(["SplitNode: N+1 entries"]) --> pickSeeds["PickSeeds:\nFind pair with MAX\nwaste area W_ij"]
    pickSeeds --> initGroups["Initialize Group G1 with seed1\nInitialize Group G2 with seed2"]
    initGroups --> checkStop{"All entries assigned?\nOR one group must\ntake remainder to\nmaintain min m?"}
    checkStop -- Yes --> splitDone(["Return G1, G2"])
    checkStop -- No --> pickNext["PickNext:\nFor each unassigned entry\ncompute d1, d2 = enlargements\nchoose entry with MAX |d1 - d2|"]
    pickNext --> assignToGroup["Assign entry to group\nwith smaller enlargement"]
    assignToGroup --> checkStop
```

> [!NOTE]
> These Mermaid diagrams are designed to be **safe-rendered**: all node IDs are alphanumeric, labels contain only plain uppercase text (no markdown formatting inside quotes), and no reserved keywords are used as node names.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define R-Tree. State any two properties of R-Tree.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** An R-Tree is a hierarchical, height-balanced spatial index structure proposed by Guttman (1984) for organizing *d*-dimensional spatial objects. Each node stores entries of the form **(MBR, pointer)**, where the MBR is the Minimum Bounding Rectangle that spatially contains all MBRs in the child node.

> **Properties (1.5 marks — any two):**
> 1. Every non-root node contains between $\lceil M/2 \rceil$ and $M$ entries.
> 2. All leaves are at the same depth (height-balanced).
> 3. The root has at least 2 children unless it is itself a leaf.
> 4. Every non-leaf entry's MBR is the smallest axis-aligned rectangle containing all child MBRs.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
**What is Minimum Bounding Rectangle (MBR)? How is the area of an MBR computed in 2D?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **MBR Definition (1.5 marks):** A Minimum Bounding Rectangle is the smallest axis-aligned rectangle that completely encloses a given set of spatial objects (or a set of MBRs). It is defined by two corner points: the lower-left $(x_{low}, y_{low})$ and upper-right $(x_{high}, y_{high})$.

> **Area Computation in 2D (1.5 marks):**
> $$ \text{Area} = (x_{high} - x_{low}) \times (y_{high} - y_{low}) $$
> Example: MBR $[2, 3, 7, 9]$ has area $(7-2) \times (9-3) = 5 \times 6 = 30$ square units.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) Explain the structure of an R-Tree with a suitable diagram. State the major operations supported. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Structure of R-Tree (4 marks):**

An R-Tree is a tree data structure similar to a B+-Tree, but for *spatial* (multi-dimensional) data. It has the following structure:

- **Root node** — either a leaf or an internal node with at least 2 children.
- **Internal nodes** — contain entries $(I, \text{child-pointer})$ where $I$ is an MBR.
- **Leaf nodes** — contain entries $(I, \text{object-id})$ pointing to the actual spatial objects.
- **Height-balanced** — all leaves at the same depth.

```
                    ROOT [MBR: 0,0 to 10,8]
                   /                        \
              [A: 0,0-5,5]              [B: 5,2-10,8]
              /          \               /          \
         [A1: R1,R2]  [A2: R3,R4]  [B1: R5,R6]  [B2: R7,R8]
```

**Major Operations (3 marks — list and one-line each):**
1. **Search / Window Query** — find all objects intersecting a query region.
2. **Insert** — add a new spatial object; may trigger node split.
3. **Delete** — remove an object; may trigger re-insertion of orphans.
4. **SplitNode** — rebalance the tree when a node overflows (Quadratic / Linear / R\*).
5. **Range Query** — find all objects within a region.

---

**(b) Construct an R-Tree (M = 3, m = 2) by inserting the rectangles in order: $R_1 = [0,0,2,2]$, $R_2 = [5,1,7,3]$, $R_3 = [1,4,3,6]$, $R_4 = [6,5,8,7]$, $R_5 = [4,0,6,2]$. Show all splits clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Insert $R_1 = [0,0,2,2]$:** Root created with $R_1$.

**Insert $R_2 = [5,1,7,3]$:** Root now has $\{R_1, R_2\}$.

**Insert $R_3 = [1,4,3,6]$:**
- Enlargement with $R_1$: MBR $[0,0,3,6]$, area = 18, $\Delta = 18-4 = 14$.
- Enlargement with $R_2$: MBR $[1,1,7,6]$, area = 36, $\Delta = 36-6 = 30$.
- Choose $R_1$'s group.
- Root has $\{R_1, R_2, R_3\}$ — **exactly 3, no overflow**.

**Insert $R_4 = [6,5,8,7]$:**
- Enlargement with $R_1$: MBR $[0,0,8,7]$, area = 56, $\Delta = 52$.
- Enlargement with $R_2$: MBR $[1,1,8,7]$, area = 49, $\Delta = 43$.
- Enlargement with $R_3$: MBR $[1,4,8,7]$, area = 28, $\Delta = 22$.
- Choose $R_3$'s group (smallest $\Delta$).
- Root would have 4 entries — **OVERFLOW! Must split.**

**Quadratic Split for $\{R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4\}$:**

Compute waste $W_{ij} = \text{Area}(\text{MBR}) - \text{Area}(E_i) - \text{Area}(E_j)$:

- $W_{1,2} = \text{Area}([0,0,7,3]) - 4 - 6 = 21 - 10 = 11$
- $W_{1,3} = \text{Area}([0,0,3,6]) - 4 - 6 = 18 - 10 = 8$
- $W_{1,4} = \text{Area}([0,0,8,7]) - 4 - 4 = 56 - 8 = 48$ ← **MAX**
- $W_{2,3} = \text{Area}([1,1,3,6]) - 6 - 6 = 12 - 12 = 0$
- $W_{2,4} = \text{Area}([1,1,8,7]) - 6 - 4 = 49 - 10 = 39$
- $W_{3,4} = \text{Area}([1,4,8,7]) - 6 - 4 = 28 - 10 = 18$

**Seeds:** $R_1$ and $R_4$ (max waste = 48).

- **Group A:** $R_1$, MBR_A = $[0,0,2,2]$.
- **Group B:** $R_4$, MBR_B = $[6,5,8,7]$.

**PickNext for $R_2$:** $d_1 = \text{Area}([0,0,7,3])-4 = 17$; $d_2 = \text{Area}([5,1,8,7])-4 = 21$. Pref = 4 → A.

**PickNext for $R_3$:** $d_1 = \text{Area}([0,0,3,6])-4 = 14$; $d_2 = \text{Area}([1,4,8,7])-4 = 24$. Pref = 10 → A.

- **Group A:** $\{R_1, R_2, R_3\}$ — 3 entries.
- **Group B:** $\{R_4\}$ — 1 entry. **VIOLATES $m = 2$!**

**Re-balance:** Force move: assign $R_3$ to B → Group B: $\{R_4, R_3\}$, 2 entries ✓. Group A: $\{R_1, R_2\}$, 2 entries ✓.

**Final Split:**
- Node A: $R_1 = [0,0,2,2]$, $R_2 = [5,1,7,3]$, MBR = $[0,0,7,3]$.
- Node B: $R_3 = [1,4,3,6]$, $R_4 = [6,5,8,7]$, MBR = $[1,4,8,7]$.

**Tree after split:**
```
                NEW ROOT
               /          \
        MBR [0,0,7,3]   MBR [1,4,8,7]
          |              |
         R1, R2         R3, R4
```

**Insert $R_5 = [4,0,6,2]$:**
- New root has 2 entries; compute enlargement.
- With Node A: new MBR $[0,0,7,3]$, area = 21, $\Delta = 21-21 = 0$.
- With Node B: new MBR $[1,0,8,7]$, area = 49, $\Delta = 49-35 = 14$.
- Choose Node A ($\Delta = 0$). Add $R_5$ to Node A.
- Node A: $\{R_1, R_2, R_5\}$, 3 entries (no overflow).

**Final R-Tree:**
```
                ROOT
               /    \
       MBR 0,0-7,3  MBR 1,4-8,7
       /     \            \
     R1,R2  R5          R3,R4
```

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]

**(a) Explain the Quadratic Split algorithm used in R-Trees. Why is it preferred over linear split? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Quadratic Split Algorithm (5 marks):**

The Quadratic Split (Guttman, 1984) divides an overflowing node of $M+1$ entries into two nodes, each containing at least $m = \lceil M/2 \rceil$ entries. It has three phases:

**Phase 1 — PickSeeds:**
- For every pair of entries $E_i, E_j$, compute the **waste**:
  $$ W_{ij} = \text{Area}(\text{MBR}(E_i \cup E_j)) - \text{Area}(E_i) - \text{Area}(E_j) $$
- Choose the pair with the **maximum** $W_{ij}$ as the seeds of the two new groups.
- Rationale: This pair is the *worst fit* if forced together, so separating them maximizes future query efficiency.

**Phase 2 — PickNext (iterative assignment):**
- For each remaining unassigned entry $E$, compute $d_1$ (enlargement needed to add $E$ to Group 1) and $d_2$ (enlargement to add to Group 2).
- Choose the entry with the **maximum** preference $\vert d_1 - d_2 \vert$ — this is the entry with the strongest preference for one group over the other.
- Assign it to the group with **smaller enlargement**.

**Phase 3 — Termination / Forced Assignment:**
- If assigning the next entry would cause one group to fall below $m$ entries, assign all remaining entries to that group (to satisfy the minimum-fill invariant) and stop.

**Why Preferred Over Linear Split (2 marks):**
1. **Better spatial distribution:** Quadratic Split considers all pairs (cost $\binom{M+1}{2}$), producing more *balanced* spatial coverage and lower MBR overlap. Linear Split examines only $2d$ boundary points.
2. **Lower overlap probability:** Empirically, Quadratic Split reduces overlap between sibling MBRs, leading to **fewer false disk accesses** during window queries.
3. **Trade-off:** Quadratic Split is slower at insertion (more CPU work) but yields a *better-shaped* tree. In disk-based systems, I/O dominates, so this is a good trade.

---

**(b) Perform a window query $Q = [2,2,6,5]$ on the final R-Tree obtained in Question A. List all rectangles intersecting $Q$ with the overlap test for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The final tree (from Q-A) is:
```
                ROOT
               /    \
       MBR [0,0,7,3]  MBR [1,4,8,7]
         |                |
       R1, R2, R5      R3, R4
```

**Step 1: Visit Root — overlap test on child MBRs with $Q = [2,2,6,5]$.**

For 2D overlap, two rectangles $A=[l_x,l_y,u_x,u_y]$ and $B=[l'_x,l'_y,u'_x,u'_y]$ overlap iff ALL four conditions hold:
$$ l_x \le u'_x \;\text{AND}\; u_x \ge l'_x \;\text{AND}\; l_y \le u'_y \;\text{AND}\; u_y \ge l'_y $$

**Test Node A MBR $[0,0,7,3]$ vs $Q=[2,2,6,5]$:**
$$ 0 \le 6 \;\checkmark,\quad 7 \ge 2 \;\checkmark,\quad 0 \le 5 \;\checkmark,\quad 3 \ge 2 \;\checkmark $$
→ **OVERLAP** → Recurse into Node A.

**Test Node B MBR $[1,4,8,7]$ vs $Q=[2,2,6,5]$:**
$$ 1 \le 6 \;\checkmark,\quad 8 \ge 2 \;\checkmark,\quad 4 \le 5 \;\checkmark,\quad 7 \ge 2 \;\checkmark $$
→ **OVERLAP** → Recurse into Node B.

**Step 2: Examine each rectangle in Node A.**

**$R_1 = [0,0,2,2]$ vs $Q=[2,2,6,5]$:**
$$ 0 \le 6 \;\checkmark,\quad 2 \ge 2 \;\checkmark,\quad 0 \le 5 \;\checkmark,\quad 2 \ge 2 \;\checkmark $$
→ **OVERLAP** ✓ (Report $R_1$)

**$R_2 = [5,1,7,3]$ vs $Q=[2,2,6,5]$:**
$$ 5 \le 6 \;\checkmark,\quad 7 \ge 2 \;\checkmark,\quad 1 \le 5 \;\checkmark,\quad 3 \ge 2 \;\checkmark $$
→ **OVERLAP** ✓ (Report $R_2$)

**$R_5 = [4,0,6,2]$ vs $Q=[2,2,6,5]$:**
$$ 4 \le 6 \;\checkmark,\quad 6 \ge 2 \;\checkmark,\quad 0 \le 5 \;\checkmark,\quad 2 \ge 2 \;\checkmark $$
→ **OVERLAP** ✓ (Report $R_5$)

**Step 3: Examine each rectangle in Node B.**

**$R_3 = [1,4,3,6]$ vs $Q=[2,2,6,5]$:**
$$ 1 \le 6 \;\checkmark,\quad 3 \ge 2 \;\checkmark,\quad 4 \le 5 \;\checkmark,\quad 6 \ge 2 \;\checkmark $$
→ **OVERLAP** ✓ (Report $R_3$)

**$R_4 = [6,5,8,7]$ vs $Q=[2,2,6,5]$:**
$$ 6 \le 6 \;\checkmark,\quad 8 \ge 2 \;\checkmark,\quad 5 \le 5 \;\checkmark,\quad 7 \ge 2 \;\checkmark $$
→ **OVERLAP** ✓ (Report $R_4$)

**Result:** All 5 rectangles ($R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4, R_5$) intersect $Q$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Disk I/O count for this query:** 1 (root) + 2 (Node A, Node B) = **3 disk accesses**. Without an R-Tree, a linear scan of 5 objects would still be 5 disk accesses. The advantage grows with $N$ — for $N=10^6$ objects, the R-Tree typically needs only $\log_m N + k/b \approx 4$ to a few dozen accesses.
